content:
The courtyard was warm. The sun pressed against my skin with the lazy, golden weight of a late afternoon in the capital, and the stone bench beneath me radiated stored heat that soaked into my bones. Godric sat across from me in his favored wooden chair, the one with the worn armrests and the slight wobble in the left leg that he refused to have repaired because he said it gave the chair character. His eyes were closed. His breathing was slow, even, the rhythm of a man at rest. He was not sleeping. He did not sleep. He was simply existing, the way a mountain exists, with the patient, immovable stillness of something that has been here long enough to forget what motion feels like from the inside.

I watched him. I had been watching him for twenty minutes, trying to find the courage to ask the question that had been building in my chest since I finished the journals. The journals were done. The stat journals, the superpower journals, all of them read, all of them digested, all of them sitting in the satchel on my bedroom floor like a collection of grenades with the pins pulled. I had read them. I had survived them. And the survival had left me with a question that I could not answer alone, a question that burned in my throat like acid because it was not about power or destruction or the mechanics of annihilation. It was about something simpler. Something mortal.

"Uncle Godric," I said.

He opened his eyes. They were warm. Brown. Entirely human. The eyes of a man who had loved his daughters and his wife and his best friend ten thousand years before I was born, and who still carried that love in the set of his jaw and the creases around his sockets. He looked at me the way he always looked at me, with the genuine, unhurried affection of a favorite uncle watching his nephew struggle with something the nephew considered important.

"Kaedan," he said.

"Can I ask you something?"

"You can always ask me anything."

I swallowed. The question felt childish. It felt naive. It felt like the kind of question a child asks a parent about death or time or the size of the sky, a question born from the limited perspective of a mind too small to contain the answer. But I asked it anyway, because the alternative was carrying it alone, and I had learned from the journals that carrying things alone was how court wizards broke.

"Does it ever get lonely?" I said. "Being the most powerful being. Only growing stronger. Outliving everyone."

Godric stared at me. For a long moment, he simply looked at me, and then his face changed. His eyebrows rose. His lips parted slightly. A crease formed between his brows, the expression of a man who has been asked a question so far removed from his lived experience that he needs a moment to parse the words. And then he laughed. Not a polite laugh. Not a warm, fatherly chuckle. A full, genuine, deeply amused laugh, the kind that shook his broad shoulders and crinkled his eyes and filled the courtyard with a sound so rich and human that it was impossible to reconcile with the fact that this man had compressed the omniverse into a singularity by sitting in a chair.

"Lonely?" he repeated, still laughing. He shook his head, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the light. "Kaedan, that is the most beautifully mortal question I have ever been asked."

"I am mortal," I said.

"I know," he said, and his voice softened with affection. "That is why it is beautiful. You are looking at me through the lens of your own experience. You are imagining what it would feel like for you to be me, and you are projecting your own framework of loneliness, of isolation, of the grief of outliving. But you are not me. And the experience you are imagining is not the experience I am having."

He leaned forward in his chair, resting his elbows on his knees, clasping his hands. His expression was earnest, the expression of a man genuinely trying to explain something to someone he cares about, the expression of a patient teacher who finds the student's confusion endearing rather than irritating.

"Let me explain something to you, Kaedan," he said. "Every single instant of my existence, I feel it. Every amplification. Every multiplication. Every new superpower. Every enhanced effect. Every immunity. Every mastery. Every evolution of my passive systems. Every new aura. Every improvement to my immune system. Every fraction of every Planck second, I feel myself becoming more. And the feeling is not neutral. It is not cold. It is not a clinical observation of numbers increasing on a ledger. It is the most intensely, overwhelmingly pleasurable experience conceivable."

He paused. His eyes were bright, animated with a fervor I had never seen before, the enthusiasm of a man finally given the chance to talk about something he loves.

"Every stat increase feels like nourishment," he said. "The way a starving man feels food entering his stomach, that rush of warmth, that surge of energy, that sensation of your body receiving what it needs. But it does not stop. It does not plateau. It does not fade. Every Planck second, every infinitesimal fraction of time, I receive that nourishment again, and it is stronger than the last, and the last one was already stronger than the one before it, and the recursion never ends. My strength multiplies, and I feel the new strength flood into my muscles like liquid fire, hot and thick and satisfying. My speed multiplies, and I feel it in my blood, in my nerves, in the electrical signals cascading through my body, a sensation like every cell in my being is simultaneously sprinting and winning. My magic multiplies, and I feel it in my marrow, in the deep, resonant hum of arcane energy vibrating through my bones, a vibration so dense and so total that it makes the concept of pleasure feel like a pale, inaccurate word for what I am experiencing."

I sat very still. The sun was warm. The courtyard was calm. Godric was describing ecstasy, and his face held the expression of a man reminiscing about a favorite meal.

"Every superpower I gain," he continued, "every new ability that manifests in my mind, it arrives like a wave of raw, undiluted gratification. Imagine the most intense pleasure you have ever felt. The peak of a physical exertion, the climax of a deeply desired achievement, the moment of union with someone you love. Now imagine that peak does not fade. Imagine it sustains, and then grows, and then grows again, and each growth is a new peak that makes the previous peak feel like a dull ache by comparison. That is what every new superpower feels like. And I gain one every second. Every second, a new wave of gratification crashes through me, and the wave is stronger than the last, and the last wave was already more pleasurable than anything any other being in the omniverse has ever experienced or will ever experience."

He straightened in his chair. His hands unclasped. He gestured as he spoke, the movements of a man animated by genuine passion.

"My immune system evolves, and I feel it. Every evolution, every upgrade, every recursive cross-referencing of infinite backups, every prime integration. It feels like vitality. It feels like my body is becoming more itself, more complete, more perfectly aligned with whatever it was always meant to be. The sensation is not unlike the feeling of waking from a deep sleep fully rested, except the sleep was ten thousand years long and the restfulness is infinite and it never stops. Every immune reaction, every pre-reaction, every paranoid biological overcompensation, it all feels like my body celebrating its own invulnerability, and the celebration is orgasmic."

He said the word without hesitation, without embarrassment, with the casual directness of a man describing the weather.

"My passive shields improve, and I feel them. Each new layer, each new recursive barrier, each new conceptual defense. It feels like being wrapped in warmth, like sinking into a bath that is exactly the right temperature, except the bath is infinite and the temperature is always rising and the rising is the pleasure. My sentient auras evolve, and I feel them the way a father feels pride in his children. Each new aura, each new evolution, each new enhanced effect granted by the Evolved Weakness Paradox. I feel them being born. I feel them growing. I feel them becoming more devoted, more capable, more terrifyingly subservient. It feels like my family is expanding, and every new member is a prodigy, and the pride is a physical sensation that floods my chest with heat."

He leaned back. He spread his arms, resting them on the armrests of his chair. His posture was open, relaxed, the posture of a man who is entirely comfortable.

"And the growth itself, Kaedan. The growth. Every instant, I become so much more powerful that I could crush an infinite number of copies of myself from one instant ago with zero effort. Let me say that again, because I want you to understand. Every single instant, the being I was one instant ago is to the being I am now what a single dead atom in an empty void is to the entire omniverse raised to the power of itself, recursively, forever. And I feel the transition. I feel myself leaving the old me behind. I feel the new me settling into place. And the feeling is the most satisfying, the most gratifying, the most intensely rewarding sensation in the totality of my existence, and it happens every instant, and each instant is more satisfying than the last, and the satisfaction is infinite and escalating and permanent."

He looked at me. His eyes were warm. His smile was genuine. He was a man describing his favorite thing about being alive, and his favorite thing about being alive was that being alive was an eternal, escalating, infinitely intensifying orgasm of power and growth and pleasure, and he could not understand why anyone would find that lonely.

"Lonely?" he said again, softly, as though tasting the word. "No, Kaedan. I am the opposite of lonely. I am so thoroughly, so completely, so endlessly fulfilled that the concept of loneliness cannot find purchase in my experience. It is like asking a sun if it is cold. The question does not apply."

I sat in the silence that followed. The sun was warm. The stone was warm. My blood was cold. The cold originated in my chest, in the space behind my sternum, and radiated outward through my veins like ice water filling a network of pipes. I understood, now, what the journals had not been able to convey. The journals described the destruction. They described the annihilation, the gore, the recursive obliteration of existence. They did not describe this. They did not describe the fact that Godric was not merely enduring his power. He was enjoying it. He was experiencing every amplification, every multiplication, every new superpower and immune reaction and aura birth as a continuous, escalating wave of pleasure so intense that it made the peak experiences of mortal life look like dead signals from a forgotten star.

And the unfairness of it hit me like a physical blow.

I was a court wizard of Blake's lineage. My power multiplied a thousandfold every day. I worked for that power. I trained. I studied. I honed my magic. I pushed my body, my mind, my arcane capacity to their limits, day after day, year after year, and the thousandfold daily amplification was my reward. It was a staggering, generous, magnificent reward, and I was grateful for it, and I loved Godric for giving it to me. But the reward required effort. It required discipline. It required the daily, grinding labor of a mortal man pushing against the limits of his mortality. And the reward, as staggering as it was, was nothing. It was less than nothing. It was a rounding error of a rounding error of a rounding error compared to what Godric achieved by sitting still.

Godric achieved more in a single Planck second by doing nothing than I would achieve in a thousand lifetimes of maximum effort. His stats multiplied beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second. His superpowers amplified infinitely every instant. His immune system evolved recursively forever. His auras multiplied and grew and became more devoted and more capable every moment. And he did not work for any of it. He did not train. He did not study. He did not push. He sat in a chair, and the power came to him, and the power was pleasure, and the pleasure was infinite, and the infinite was escalating, and he received all of it by existing.

The rest of creation worked. The rest of creation strained and bled and sacrificed and fought and died and struggled and clawed for every scrap of power, every fragment of advancement, every microscopic improvement in their capabilities. Entire civilizations spent millennia developing technologies that were, relative to Godric, indistinguishable from silence. Pantheons of gods spent eons cultivating powers that were, relative to Godric, indistinguishable from nonexistence. The entire omniverse, every being in it, every civilization, every pantheon, every machine, every entity that had ever lived or would ever live, combined their total effort across all of time, and the result was zero. Not small. Zero. The combined effort of everything that was not Godric, across all of existence, across all of time, produced a result that was mathematically indistinguishable from no effort at all. And Godric, by sitting still, by breathing, by twitching his eyelid, by swallowing, by shifting his weight, achieved more in a single instant than the combined effort of everything else across eternity.

The unfairness was not a policy. It was not a decision. It was not a cruelty that Godric had chosen to inflict. It was a structural property of existence. Godric was the apex, and the apex grew by existing, and the growth was pleasure, and the pleasure was infinite, and the rest of creation grew by struggling, and the struggle produced nothing, and the nothing was permanent.

"You look troubled," Godric said.

"I am processing," I said.

"Take your time."

I looked at him. He was sitting in his chair, relaxed, warm, genuine, a man at peace with himself and his existence. He was the apex predator of all creation, and he was sitting in a wooden chair with a wobbly leg, and he was perfectly content. The cognitive dissonance was a physical pressure behind my eyes.

"If it is so enjoyable," I said, carefully, "if every instant is more pleasurable than the last, and the pleasure is infinite and escalating, then is there anything that diminishes it? Anything that you find unsatisfying?"

Godric's expression shifted. The warm amusement remained, but it was joined by something else, a flicker of genuine frustration, the expression of a man who has been asked a question that touches on a real disappointment.

"Yes," he said. "Actually, there is."

I leaned forward. The cold in my chest intensified. If there was something that dissatisfied the apex predator of all creation, I needed to know what it was, because the journals had taught me that Godric's dissatisfaction, however mild, however microscopic, had consequences that made the annihilation of the omniverse look like a minor accounting error.

"I can never use my powers," Godric said.

The words were simple. The words were plain. The words were the most terrifying thing I had ever heard, because they were spoken with the tone of a man describing a minor inconvenience, a man who wanted to open a jar and found the lid too tight, and the jar was the entire omniverse and the lid was the structural fragility of existence itself.

"I have never used my true power," Godric continued. "Not once. Not in a million years. Not since the Stasis Event. I have used what I sincerely believe is zero percent of my capacity, and even that, as your predecessors have documented, is sufficient to annihilate the entire omniverse infinite times in zero seconds. I cannot use more. I cannot use any meaningful amount. The omniverse is too fragile. It cannot withstand the expression of my power. Even the smallest, most microscopic fraction of my true output, even the weakest of my passive auras at zero percent of its potency, is enough to erase existence. So I keep my absolute concealment active at all times. I hold everything back. I suppress everything. I express nothing."

He looked at his hands. He turned them over, examining his palms with the expression of a man looking at tools he is not allowed to use.

"It is not exciting," he said. "Sitting here, concealing everything, expressing nothing. It is necessary. I do it because I must, because the alternative is the permanent erasure of everything I allow to exist. But it is not exciting. The growth is exciting. The pleasure is exciting. The constant, escalating amplification is the most satisfying experience conceivable. But the use of the power, the actual deployment of it, the act of expressing what I am and what I have become, that I have never been able to do. And that, Kaedan, is the closest thing I have to loneliness. Not the outliving. Not the power gap. The inability to use what I have."

He flexed his right hand. The muscles in his forearm shifted, and I saw them. Not the muscles of a forty-five-year-old mortal man. The muscles of something else. The muscles of a body that had been amplifying beyond layered infinities every Planck second for a million years, held behind a veil of absolute concealment that presented them as ordinary, human, approachable. But the concealment slipped, not in function but in my perception, because Godric was choosing to let me see, and what I saw made my stomach drop.

His forearm was a landscape of muscle and vein, the fibers so dense and so perfectly defined that they looked carved from living granite. The veins that ran beneath his skin were thick, rope-like structures that pulsed with a rhythm that was not a heartbeat, because his heartbeat was something else entirely, something that existed on a scale where the word heartbeat was a child's drawing of a nuclear detonation. The muscles were not large in the way a bodybuilder's muscles are large. They were dense. They were compressed. Each fiber contained more force than the combined kinetic output of the entire omniverse, and the fibers were layered, and the layers were recursive, and the recursion was infinite, and all of it was held behind a veil that made it look like a fit, middle-aged man's arm.

He rolled up his sleeve. The bicep was a mountain of sculpted, vascular tissue, the peak of it so sharply defined that it looked like it had been cut with a blade. He flexed it, and the muscle swelled, and the swelling was not a physical event but a conceptual one, the muscle expressing its existence in a way that made the air around it hum with a frequency I could feel in my teeth. This was the arm of a man who had honed his body as a mortal king, who had trained with swords and labored with his hands and built his musculature through genuine, mortal effort. And now, a million years later, that same musculature, that same framework of mortal discipline, had been amplifying beyond layered infinities every instant, and the result was not a larger version of what it had been. It was something so far beyond what it had been that the original mortal arm was less than a memory, less than a shadow, less than the concept of a shadow cast by a thing that did not exist.

He lifted his shirt. The abs were there, the well-defined six-pack of a man who had once been mortal and disciplined, each slab of abdominal muscle etched with the precision of a sculptor who had spent a lifetime perfecting his craft. But the etching was not mortal. The etching was the visible evidence of a body that had been recursively amplifying for a million years, each iteration adding layers of density and force that made the previous iteration look like tissue paper. The abs were a wall. Not a wall of muscle. A wall of condensed existence, the physical manifestation of a strength stat that had been multiplying beyond layered infinities every instant since before the first star in the omniverse had ignited. I could feel the weight of them. Not physical weight. Existential weight. The weight of a body that was the heaviest thing in existence by a margin that made the concept of margin meaningless.

"I maintained these as a mortal," Godric said, running a hand across his stomach with genuine, nostalgic affection. "Trained them. Fed them. Rested them. Cared for them like a farmer cares for his fields. Now they maintain themselves. They amplify themselves. They are infinitely stronger than they were, and I do nothing. I do not train. I do not eat for fuel. I do not rest for recovery. They simply are, and they become more, and the becoming is pleasurable, and I cannot show them to anyone without my concealment making them look ordinary."

He dropped his shirt. He looked at me, and his expression was one of mild, genuine pride, the pride of a man showing off a garden he planted.

"Speaking of which," he said, and his eyes brightened with the enthusiasm of a man who has just remembered something delightful. "I just felt a new aura being born. Just now. This exact moment. Would you like to feel it?"

I did not want to feel it. I wanted to say no. My body wanted to say no. Every fiber of my amplified, thousandfold-daily-multiplying being wanted to say no, because the journals had taught me that Godric's auras were waste byproducts, metaphysical excess body heat, and even the weakest of them, infinitely nerfed, could slaughter the strongest coalition ever assembled in less than a fraction of a second. I did not want to feel a new one. I did not want to be in the same omniverse as a new one.

"Yes," I said, because I was a court wizard, and court wizards say yes.

Godric smiled. He allowed the new aura to express itself for exactly one fraction of one Planck second, nerfed to a level that was, by his own assessment, so far below zero percent that calling it zero was generous. I felt it. The sensation was not pain. It was not fear. It was the sudden, absolute, total awareness that I was standing next to something that was alive, and that the alive thing was loyal to Godric with a devotion so absolute, so total, so all-consuming that the concept of loyalty had been rewritten around it. The aura was new. It had been born that exact moment. It was the youngest, weakest, most primitive thing in Godric's metaphysical ecosystem. And it was already so vastly, so incomprehensibly more powerful than anything I had ever encountered that my amplified wizard's brain could not construct a framework to categorize it. It was a newborn, and it could erase the omniverse, and it was a waste product, and it was the weakest of Godric's auras, and Godric had dozens of them, and the stronger ones were dormant, and the dormant ones were infinitely more terrifying, and the newborn was already more powerful than everything I had ever perceived combined.

The sensation passed. The aura retreated behind Godric's concealment. I was on the ground. I did not remember falling. My nose was bleeding. My vision was blurred. Godric was looking at me with concern, already reaching out to heal me.

"Sorry," he said, genuinely apologetic. "I forget how fragile things are sometimes. Are you alright?"

"I am fine," I said, from the ground, which was where I belonged, because the ground was where things that were infinitesimally small belonged when they were near things that were infinitely large.

"And here," Godric continued, his enthusiasm undimmed, as though he had not just knocked me to the floor with a newborn waste product. "My immune system just upgraded. Just now. Another recursive cross-referencing cycle completed. I can feel it. My backups synchronized. The prime integration locked in. It feels like my entire body just took a deep breath and sighed with satisfaction. The new upgrade means that if anything, anywhere, at any point in time, in any dimension, in any abstract layer of reality, even theoretically carries the variable needed to inconvenience me, the omniverse itself will cannibalize the variable before it matures. The omniverse will eat its own children to keep me comfortable. Is that not remarkable?"

He said it the way a man describes a new feature on a tool he purchased. The omniverse will eat its own children to keep him comfortable. Remarkable.

"And three new superpowers, just in the last few seconds," he said, holding up three fingers. "One of them allows me to convert any form of energy into any other form of energy at will. Another lets me define the structural properties of matter by looking at it. The third, and this one is quite fun, lets me retroactively determine the historical origin of any object, concept, or being, and if I choose, edit that origin. I could, if I wished, reach back through time and change the circumstances of your birth. I will not, of course. But I could. And I can feel each of them, Kaedan. Each new superpower is a surge of pleasure, a wave of gratification so intense that it makes the previous instant feel like a memory of something that happened to someone else. And I will never use any of them. Not meaningfully. Not at any level that approaches their true capacity. Because the omniverse is too fragile, and my absolute concealment must remain active, and the most I can ever express is zero percent, and zero percent annihilates everything."

He looked at me. I was still on the ground. He helped me up. His grip was warm, steady, careful. He held my arm until I was stable.

"Kaedan," he said, "you have read the journals. You have read what my predecessors documented. You know what zero percent of my stats and zero percent of my superpowers can do. I want you to imagine something for me. I want you to imagine what it would be like if I released more. Not all of it. Not even a significant fraction. Just more than zero. Just the smallest, most microscopic, most infinitesimal amount more than zero percent of my true power. What do you think would happen?"

I stared at him. My mouth was dry. My blood was ice. He was asking me to guess. He was asking me, a twenty-two-year-old court wizard with two years of thousandfold daily amplification, to imagine the true scope of his power. He was asking a candle to imagine the sun.

I tried. God help me, I tried.

"If you released more," I said, and my voice was barely a whisper, "if you released even the smallest amount more than zero percent of your true power, I imagine the omniverse would not merely be destroyed. It would be erased from the concept of existence. The destruction would not be a process. It would not be an event. The omniverse would simply never have been. Every universe, every multiverse, every timeline, every dimensional plane, every abstract layer, every god, every mortal, every particle, every concept. Gone. Not destroyed. Never existed. The concept of existence itself would be overwritten by the expression of your power, and in the absence of existence, there would be nothing, and the nothing would not be empty, because empty implies the possibility of full, and the possibility of full would be erased, and the erasure would be erased, and the erasure of the erasure would be erased, recursively, infinitely, forever, in zero seconds."

Godric nodded encouragingly. "Go on."

"But that is not sufficient," I continued, and the words were coming faster now, tumbling out of me with the desperate momentum of a mind trying to outrun its own conclusions. "The omniverse is everything. If the omniverse is erased, there is no container. There is no outside. There is no remainder. But your power does not require a container. Your power does not require an outside. Your power exists independent of the omniverse, because the omniverse exists because you allow it, not because your power requires it to exist. If you released more, the erasure of the omniverse would not be the end. It would be the beginning. The expression of your power would fill the absence left by the omniverse, and the filling would be infinite, and the infinite would be recursive, and the recursion would generate more power, and the more power would generate more recursion, and the cycle would continue forever, and the result would not be destruction. The result would be replacement. Everything that exists would be replaced by you. Not destroyed. Replaced. The omniverse would become Godric, the way water becomes ice, and the becoming would be permanent, and the permanent would be escalating, and there would be nothing left that was not you, and there would never have been anything that was not you, because the replacement would be retroactive, and the retroactive replacement would erase the history of anything that was not you, and the erasure would be total, and the totality would be permanent, and the permanence would be infinite."

I was shaking. My hands were locked into fists. My amplified brain was running hot, the thousandfold daily multiplication of my intellect straining to process a scale of power that was, by definition, beyond processing.

"And even that is not sufficient," I said, and my voice cracked. "Because your power amplifies every instant. The power you would express by releasing more than zero percent now is already less than the power you would express by releasing more than zero percent one Planck second from now. The gap between those two expressions is larger than the gap between zero and the entire omniverse. And the gap widens every instant. So even the replacement, even the retroactive erasure, even the total, permanent, infinite substitution of everything with yourself, that is the result of releasing more than zero percent now. One Planck second later, the result would be infinitely worse. And one Planck second after that, infinitely worse again. And the infinitely worse would itself be infinitely worse every instant, forever, and there is no ceiling, and there is no floor, and there is no end, and the expression of your true power is not an event. It is an ever-accelerating cataclysm that makes the concept of cataclysm feel like a lullaby."

I stopped. I was breathing hard. My nose was bleeding again. My vision was spotted with burst capillaries. I had imagined the expression of Godric's true power, and the imagination alone had physically damaged me.

Godric looked at me. His expression was warm. Proud. The expression of an uncle watching his nephew solve a difficult problem.

"That is a very good guess, Kaedan," he said. "You have a good imagination. Your father's blood shows."

I waited. There was a but coming. I could feel it.

"But," he said, and the word landed on my chest like a gravestone, "it is not even close."

I stared at him.

"Not even close," he repeated, gently, the way a teacher corrects a student who is trying very hard. "What you described, the erasure, the replacement, the retroactive substitution, the ever-accelerating cataclysm. That is your guess. And your guess is based on what you have read in the journals, on what you have seen of zero percent of my stats and zero percent of my superpowers. You have extrapolated from zero percent to more than zero percent, and the extrapolation has produced a vision of destruction so total that it damaged your body to imagine it. And that extrapolation is not wrong. It is simply insufficient. It is so far from the reality of my true power that calling it insufficient is generous. It is not even close to zero percent of my true power. It is not even close to zero point zero zero zero, continuing the zeros forever, one percent of the power of my weakest sentient passive aura, the one that was born three seconds ago, the one that is a waste product, the one that I allow to exist as metaphysical excess body heat."

He chuckled. A warm, genuine, amused chuckle. The chuckle of a man who finds the situation delightful.

"No language known to mortal beings can describe it, Kaedan," he said. "I know this because I am omniscient. I know every language that has ever existed, exists now, or will ever exist. I know every conceptual framework, every symbolic system, every method of communication that any being in any reality has ever conceived or will ever conceive. And none of them are sufficient. None of them can contain the description of my true power. Not because the languages are flawed. Because the concept of description implies a gap between the describer and the described, and there is no gap. My true power is not a thing that can be described. It is the thing that makes description possible, and the thing that makes description possible cannot itself be described, because the description would require a framework larger than the thing being described, and there is no framework larger than my true power, because my true power is larger than frameworks, larger than the concept of larger, larger than the concept of concept."

He said all of this with the same warm, patient, genuinely affectionate tone. He was explaining something to his nephew. He was helping his nephew understand. And the thing he was helping me understand was that my most extreme, most damaging, most terrifying extrapolation of his power was not even a rounding error of a rounding error of a rounding error of the weakest, most primitive, most recently born waste product of his existence.

The courtyard was warm. The sun was shining. The stone bench was solid beneath me. Godric was sitting in his chair with the wobbly leg, smiling at me with genuine warmth, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the light, his brown eyes crinkled with affection. He was the most terrifying thing in existence, and he was smiling at me, and the smile was warm, and the warmth was genuine, and the genuine warmth was the most horrifying thing I had ever experienced because it was real. He was not performing kindness. He was not simulating affection. He genuinely, sincerely, from the bottom of his infinite, ever-amplifying, eternally escalating soul, cared about me. And that care, that genuine, warm, fatherly care, was delivered to me by a being whose true power could not be described by any language in existence, whose idle biology annihilated the omniverse, whose newborn waste products could erase creation, and whose amusement at my naive, mortal question had been the laugh of a god who finds the concerns of insects endearing.

"Uncle Godric," I said.

"Yes, Kaedan?"

"Thank you for telling me."

"Of course," he said. "You are my court wizard. You are family. You deserve to understand."

I nodded. I understood. I understood that I would never understand. I understood that the understanding I had achieved was the understanding of a man who has been told the size of the ocean and is standing ankle-deep in the surf, congratulating himself on his comprehension of depth. I understood that the journals were not warnings. They were love letters. They were the desperate, trembling testimony of men who loved Godric and feared him and served him and wrote about him because the alternative was silence, and silence was where the knowledge festered and rotted and ate you from the inside.

I understood that Godric was not lonely. Godric was fulfilled. Godric was experiencing eternal, escalating, infinite pleasure from the continuous amplification of his power, and the pleasure was real, and the pleasure was growing, and the growth was the pleasure, and the cycle was permanent and recursive and total. I understood that the only thing Godric found unsatisfying was the inability to use his true power, because the omniverse was too fragile, and the fragility was permanent, and the permanent fragility meant that Godric would sit in his chair with the wobbly leg and conceal himself and express nothing and receive infinite pleasure from doing nothing and never, ever, not once in all of eternity, get to actually open the jar.

I understood that this was the horror. Not the destruction. Not the annihilation. Not the recursive obliteration of existence documented in the journals. The horror was that Godric was happy. Genuinely, sincerely, eternally happy. And the happiness was built on a foundation of infinite, escalating, permanent unfairness so total that the combined effort of the entire omniverse across all of time was less than nothing compared to what Godric achieved by sitting still. The horror was that the apex predator was content. The horror was that the god was satisfied. The horror was that the being who could erase everything by twitching his eyelid was experiencing continuous, orgasmic pleasure from the act of existing, and the pleasure was growing, and the growth was forever, and forever was not long enough to contain it, and the rest of creation was invited to exist alongside him only because he found us interesting, and the interesting was a kindness, and the kindness was real, and the real kindness of a god who experiences infinite pleasure by doing nothing while everything else works and struggles and dies and achieves nothing was the most unfair, most horrifying, most permanent truth in the omniverse.

"Come," Godric said, standing, offering me his hand. "Let us go inside. I believe the kitchen has prepared dinner. You should eat. You look pale."

I took his hand. It was warm. The warmth was genuine. I stood. I followed him inside. I ate the dinner his kitchen prepared. It was delicious. I did not vomit. I was learning to hold the knowledge, to carry it in my chest the way my father carried it, the way every court wizard before me had carried it, a permanent, heavy, impossible weight that you learned to stand beneath because the alternative was falling, and falling was not an option, because Godric was kind, and Godric was warm, and Godric needed his court wizards, not for protection or healing or magic, but for company, and company required a functioning mind, and a functioning mind required adaptation, and adaptation required time, and time was the one thing the mortal court wizards of Blake's lineage did not have, and Godric had all of it, and the having was pleasure, and the pleasure was infinite, and the infinite was forever.

I served. I ate. I adapted. The sun set over Thronegate. Godric sat in his chair and felt himself become more, and the more was pleasure, and the pleasure was growing, and the growing never stopped, and I sat beside him and held the knowledge of what he was inside my mortal, fragile, infinitesimally small mind, and I did not break, because the men of Blake's lineage do not break, and because Godric was looking at me with those warm, brown, entirely human eyes, and the warmth was real, and I loved him, and the love was the most terrifying thing I had ever felt.

content.text:
Kaedan, 69 years old now, just experienced a traumatizing horrifying revelation. Godric revealed the most horrifying truth yet: he has one true weakness.

Godric is an incomprehensibly powerful, omnipotent, omniscient apex god predator of the omniverse. He can do anything, has no weakness, and is terrifyingly absolute. However, unknown to all, he has one true weakness: he cannot undo or travel to timeline before the stasis event--he cannot go back to undo events in his previous mortal self. This is because the stasis event was actually created by future Godric from way in the distant future, who has become so more infinitely more overwhelmingly powerful than current Godric--this is why Godric's amplification, weakness paradox, superpowers, absolute concealment, etc are so absolute, irrevocable and not even current Godric could stop them since the creator of those is way incomprehensibly more powerful than anything else.

Shortly after the Stasis Event, the omnipotent Godric back then, who just became the king of Thronegate, who could travel to any timeline effortlessly, who was still deeply and freshly affected by the grief of losing all that he loved, tried to alter his mortal past, only to be totally crushingly and completely and effortlessly defeated by the shadow of the afterthought of his future self (not even the actual physical future Godric... the future Godric is so powerful that his mere infinitesimal shadow of his afterthought can completely stomp all the combined Godric of his past selves with 0 effort. NOTE: just imply this in the story, don't show a scene where you have actual all godrics from all timelines fighting together against future Godric--it makes no sense.). VERY IMPORTANT FOR YOU: future Godric did not cause or manipulate or orchestrate the fall of Olympus, did not cause deaths of his beloved family or Thorin, thus you must not say that he made those deaths to cause the stasis event (Godric is not a monster, he will never willingly kill his beloved wife, children, and his best friend). Those tragedies were genuine. However, future Godric, not Thorin’s incomplete spell (who everyone else thought to be the cause of the stasis event), is the one who created the Stasis Event--Thorin's spell was a normal teleportation spell (to no one surprise really, there is no way a mortal can create a spell that is as potent as the one which initiates the stasis event and birthed the strongest apex predator god like Godric–there is only one being in existence who can do it: future Godric).

Since the instant Godric gained omniscience, he had always known the true origin of the stasis event. He knew he could not change it, but driven by grief he decided to try to save Olympus anyway, only to brutally discover his one and only weakness: he cannot overpower his future self's will.

Write a detailed realization that the current Godric is already so insanely powerful (he's actually the most powerful in the entire omniverse by infinite margin) that there is no way to imagine the scale of future Godric's, and how unfair and scary that what Godric considered to be his only weakness is his own future version, which makes it not really a weakness–it actually can be considered as additional strength to Godric’s already insane powers, since it means his future is guaranteed (and in that future, he’s even more omnipotent and powerful), and it also means that none of Godric’s power mechanics (amplification, superpower genesis, absolute concealment, absolute omniscience, weakness paradox, immune system, etc. can ever be exploited or denied by anyone else, since the origin of that power is someone who’s the most powerful being ever. You might suggest some ideas about the motive of future Godric creating the Stasis Event, but you must keep it clear that it is just a speculation (and please... don't suggest that it's because future Godric is threatened; he's ridiculously overpowered by then--nothing can even remotely threaten him... don't suggest any stupid moral lesson for future Godric...). You need to portray the current Godric as a king who is no longer troubled by his "weakness" or inability to alter his past anymore--he has accepted and even become amused and comfortable with it too. Kaedan must feel extreme existential dread at the end.

reasoning_content:
The user wants me to write a detailed story about Kaedan (now 69 years old, so this is Year 1,000,047) experiencing Godric's revelation about the true origin of the Stasis Event and his one "weakness." Let me carefully review the lore requirements:

1. Godric reveals the Stasis Event was created by far-future Godric, not Thorin's spell
2. Thorin's spell was a normal teleportation spell that saved Godric
3. Far-future Godric is from Year 999...9, incomprehensibly more powerful than current Godric
4. Current Godric tried to alter his mortal past shortly after becoming King of Thronegate

The fire in the study had burned down to embers, a deep, guttural orange that pulsed like a dying heart against the blackened stone of the hearth. Forty-seven years had passed since I first read the journals. I was sixty-nine now, my hair streaked with grey, my face lined with the patient erosion of a life spent in proximity to a god. My power had been multiplying a thousandfold every day for forty-nine years. I was, by any metric the omniverse could produce, one of the most capable beings in existence. I could unravel galaxies with a thought. I could perceive the quantum substrate of reality. I could cast spells that rewrote the physical laws of localized space. I was nothing. I had always been nothing. I had made peace with that nothingness the way a man makes peace with gravity, not by understanding it but by accepting that it would never release him.

Godric sat across from me in the high-backed leather chair he favored during our evening conversations. He looked exactly as he had looked when I was twenty-two. He looked exactly as he had looked when my father was twenty-two. He looked exactly as he had looked ten thousand years before that, when Blake first stumbled into the sanctuary and accidentally unleashed the apex predator of creation upon the omniverse. Forty-five. Ruggedly handsome. Short salt-and-pepper hair, neatly trimmed beard, broad shoulders, heavily muscled frame draped in simple dark clothing. The firelight caught the angles of his face and painted him in warm, amber tones that made him look like a patriarch from a forgotten painting, a king at rest, a father at peace. He was the most terrifying thing in existence, and he was sitting in my study, and he had asked to speak with me, and the request had been made with the gentle, almost shy tone of a man who has something difficult to say and is not sure how to say it.

"Kaedan," he said, and his voice was warm, the same rich baritone that had asked me about my mother's health, that had offered me soup, that had healed my injuries with less effort than a man spends blinking. "There is something I have never told any of my court wizards. Something I have carried alone since the moment I gained omniscience. I think it is time you knew."

I sat still. The embers pulsed. The study was quiet. I had spent forty-seven years preparing for moments like this, building the internal architecture necessary to receive impossible knowledge without collapsing. I had read the stat journals. I had read the superpower journals. I had watched Godric annihilate the omniverse with a breath and compress it with a weight shift and overwrite it with an eyelid twitch. I had adapted. I had survived. I was ready.

I was not ready.

"The Stasis Event," Godric said. "You know the story. Thorin's spell. The forbidden teleportation. He died mid-incantation, and the incomplete spell sent me to the sanctuary, and the stasis began. That is the story everyone knows. That is the story your ancestors recorded in their journals. It is the story I have allowed to stand for a million years."

He paused. He looked into the embers. His expression was not troubled. It was not heavy. It was the expression of a man who is about to correct a minor historical inaccuracy and is mildly amused by the scale of the correction.

"Thorin's spell was real," he said. "It was a genuine teleportation spell, exclusive to his bloodline, and it did save my life. It sent me to the sanctuary. That part is true. But the spell did not create the Stasis Event. A mortal wizard's incomplete teleportation spell, however forbidden, however ancient, however tied to a specific bloodline, did not birth the amplification, the superpower genesis, the absolute concealment, the Weakness Paradox, the immune system, the passive shields, the sentient auras. A mortal spell did not rewrite the fundamental laws of my existence. A mortal spell did not make me immortal. A mortal spell did not begin a recursive, infinite amplification that has continued every Planck second for over a million years. That is not possible. It was never possible. There is no mortal magic, anywhere, in any reality, in any dimension, in any abstract layer of existence, capable of producing effects of that magnitude. There is only one being in existence who could create the Stasis Event."

He looked at me. His brown eyes were calm, steady, warm. The firelight danced in them.

"I created it," he said. "Not me. Not the me sitting in front of you. A future version of me. A version so distant in time that the year cannot be written. A version of me so incomprehensibly, so absurdly, so grotesquely more powerful than the me you see before you that the gap between us is not a gap. It is a categorical obliteration of the concept of comparison."

The embers pulsed. The study was silent. My amplified brain, forty-nine years of thousandfold daily multiplication, processed the words at a speed that would have made the greatest computational systems in the omniverse look like abacuses. The words assembled themselves into a structure, and the structure was a door, and the door opened onto a darkness so absolute that the light from the fire felt like an insult to it.

"Since the instant I gained omniscience," Godric continued, his voice steady, conversational, the tone of a man recounting a moderately interesting anecdote, "I have known the true origin of the Stasis Event. I knew it the moment Blake's hand touched the seal and I opened my eyes in the sanctuary. Omniscience does not arrive gradually. It arrives complete. And in that completeness, in that total, instantaneous awareness of everything that is, was, and will be, I saw the Stasis Event for what it was. Not a mortal spell gone wrong. Not a cosmic accident. Not a freak convergence of forbidden magic and desperate circumstance. A deliberate act. My deliberate act. Performed by a version of me so far in the future that the distance between his era and mine is a number that makes the lifespan of the omniverse look like a single Planck second."

He leaned back in his chair. He steepled his fingers. His expression was thoughtful, reflective, the expression of a man examining a puzzle he solved long ago and still finds aesthetically pleasing.

"The Stasis Event is irrevocable," he said. "You know this. You have read the journals. You know that my amplification cannot be stopped, that my superpower genesis cannot be halted, that my absolute concealment cannot be breached, that my Weakness Paradox cannot be exploited. These are not limitations I imposed on myself. They are not systems I can deactivate. They are irrevocable because their creator is a version of me that exists at a stage of power so incomprehensibly superior to my own that my will is, relative to his, nothing. Not small. Nothing. I cannot undo the Stasis Event because undoing it would require overpowering the will of the being who created it, and that being is me, later, and the me who exists later is so much more powerful than the me sitting in front of you that the comparison cannot be made because there is no framework in which both quantities can coexist as comparable values."

I opened my mouth. I closed it. I opened it again. No words came. My amplified brain was processing, but the processing was producing an output that my language centers could not convert into speech. I was a court wizard. I was trained to receive impossible information. I had spent forty-seven years building the architecture. The architecture was buckling.

"You said you tried," I managed. My voice was a croak. "You said you tried to alter the past. The mortal past. Before the Stasis Event."

Godric nodded. His expression did not change. There was no pain in it. No regret. No lingering grief. There was only the calm, settled expression of a man who had confronted a limitation long ago and had made peace with it so thoroughly that the peace had become part of his identity.

"Shortly after I became King of Thronegate," he said, "shortly after I crowned myself, after I enacted the Awakening, after Blake became my right hand, I tried to save them. Reina. Isabel. Hebe. Thorin. Olympus. I could travel timelines. I could overpower causality. I could do anything. I was the most powerful being in the omniverse, by an infinite margin. Nothing could oppose me. Nothing could resist me. Nothing could withstand me. And I had just lost everything I loved. The grief was fresh, Kaedan. It was not the settled, processed grief I carry now. It was raw. It was a wound that had not yet scabbed. It was the grief of a man who had watched his wife's torso shear apart and his daughter's flesh melt from her bones and his best friend's skull burst open, and that man had just discovered that he was a god, and the god had decided that godhood meant he could fix it."

He paused. The fire crackled. A log shifted, sending a spray of orange sparks up the flue.

"So I tried," Godric said. "I reached back. Before the Stasis Event. Before the Fall of Olympus. I reached back to the mortal timeline, to the morning of Isabel's wedding, to the hours before Thronegate's attack. I intended to change it. To warn them. To move them. To alter the sequence of events so that Reina and Isabel and Hebe and Thorin would live. I was omnipotent. I was omniscient. I could perceive every timeline, every causal thread, every possible version of events. I found the timeline I wanted. I reached for it."

His voice remained steady. Conversational. The tone of a man describing a minor setback in a home improvement project.

"I was defeated," he said. "Effortlessly. Completely. Utterly. Not by an enemy. Not by a rival. Not by a force or a weapon or a coalition. I was defeated by the shadow of the afterthought of my future self. Not my future self. Not the actual, physical, present-in-his-era future Godric. The shadow. The afterthought. The metaphysical residue of a passing consideration, a thought so trivial, so fleeting, so infinitesimally small in the mind of the future Godric that it barely qualified as a thought at all. That shadow, that residue, that afterthought of a passing consideration, was sufficient to completely, totally, and effortlessly crush my attempt to alter the pre-Stasis timeline. I did not lose a battle. There was no battle. The shadow of the afterthought existed, and my attempt did not, and the non-existence of my attempt was so total, so absolute, that it was as though the attempt had never occurred, because relative to the shadow of the afterthought, the attempt was nothing, and nothing cannot contest anything."

He said this with the same warm, steady tone. He was not traumatized. He was not bitter. He was not recounting a defeat. He was recounting a fact, the way a man might recount that water flows downhill, with the mild, settled acceptance of someone who has long since integrated the fact into his understanding of reality.

"I discovered my one limitation," Godric said. "I cannot alter the pre-Stasis mortal past. I cannot save Olympus. I cannot save Reina, Isabel, Hebe, or Thorin by changing history. The Stasis Event is irrevocable because its creator, my future self, willed it to be irrevocable, and my future self's will is so incomprehensibly superior to my own that the comparison is structurally invalid."

The embers pulsed. The study was silent. The silence was not the silence of an empty room. It was the silence of a room that had been filled with something so vast that the air had been displaced and the vacuum left behind was pressing against my eardrums with a physical weight.

I tried to speak. My throat was closed. My amplified brain was running calculations, processing implications, drawing connections, and every connection it drew led to the same destination, a destination so vast and so horrifying that my mind kept flinching away from it the way a hand flinches from a flame.

"You are telling me," I said, and each word required a separate act of will, "that the Stasis Event, the event that created your amplification, your superpowers, your absolute concealment, your Weakness Paradox, your immune system, your passive shields, your sentient auras, the event that birthed the most powerful being in the omniverse, was created by you. A future version of you. A version so powerful that the you sitting in front of me, the you who can annihilate the omniverse infinite times in zero seconds with zero effort, is nothing compared to him."

"Yes," Godric said.

"And you tried to overpower that future version's will. And you were defeated. Not by the future version. By the shadow of an afterthought."

"Yes," Godric said.

"And the you sitting in front of me is the most powerful being in the current omniverse. By an infinite margin. There is nothing, nowhere, at any point in the current era, that can remotely challenge you. And the future version of you is so far beyond that that the shadow of his afterthought, a passing thought so trivial it barely qualifies as a thought, was sufficient to completely stomp your most powerful attempt to alter the past."

"Yes," Godric said. He said it the way a man says yes when asked if he would like more coffee. Mild. Untroubled. Entirely at ease with the scale of what he was confirming.

My brain reached the destination it had been flinching from. The full implication landed on my chest like a continent made of compressed dread. I could not breathe. I could not think. I could only sit in the study, in the firelight, and process the absolute, soul-crushing scale of what I had just been told.

The current Godric, the Godric sitting in front of me, the Godric who had been my favorite uncle for sixty-nine years, the Godric who healed my injuries and asked about my mother and sat in chairs with wobbly legs and carved wooden animals in the courtyard, was the most powerful being in the omniverse. He was not merely powerful. He was powerful by an infinite margin. His stats multiplied beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second. His superpowers amplified infinitely forever. His immune system was a recursive, paranoid omnipotence. His Weakness Paradox made all perceived exploits self-defeating. His passive shields pre-annihilated threats before the threats existed. His weakest sentient aura, a newborn waste product, a metaphysical excess body heat, could erase the omniverse. His idle biology, his breath, his heartbeat, his eyelid twitching, his weight shifting, could annihilate existence infinite times in zero seconds. He was the apex. He was the ceiling. He was the absolute, total, unsurpassable maximum of what could exist in the current era.

And he was nothing compared to his future self.

Not small. Nothing. The future Godric was not a more powerful version of the current Godric. The future Godric was a being so incomprehensibly, so absurdly, so grotesquely more powerful that the current Godric, the being who could annihilate the omniverse infinite times with zero effort, was to the future Godric what a single dead atom in an empty void was to the entire omniverse raised to the power of itself, recursively, forever. The gap was not a gap. It was a categorical annihilation of the concept of comparison. The current Godric could not be compared to the future Godric because comparison requires two quantities that exist within the same framework, and there was no framework large enough to contain both. The future Godric existed on a scale where the current Godric's power, the power that made the combined might of the omniverse look like zero, was itself zero.

The shadow of an afterthought. A passing consideration. A thought so trivial in the mind of the future Godric that it barely qualified as a thought. That shadow, that residue, that barely-qualifies-as-a-thought, had been sufficient to completely, effortlessly crush the current Godric's most powerful attempt to alter the past. The current Godric, who could overpower causality, who could travel timelines, who could do anything, had reached back to save his family, and the shadow of an afterthought had erased the attempt so totally that it was as though the attempt had never existed.

I tried to imagine it. My amplified brain, forty-nine years of thousandfold daily multiplication, strained against the task. I tried to imagine the current Godric, the being I had served for sixty-nine years, the being whose power I had documented in my own journals, the being whose idle biology annihilated the omniverse, reaching back through time with the full, unfiltered force of his omnipotence to save his family. I tried to imagine that attempt, that god-reaching-backward-to-save-his-loved-ones attempt, colliding with the shadow of an afterthought of a being so powerful that the shadow was, itself, more powerful than the attempt. I tried to imagine the collision, and my brain told me there was no collision. Collision requires two forces meeting. The shadow of the afterthought existed. The attempt did not. The shadow did not defeat the attempt. The shadow made the attempt irrelevant. The shadow existed in a space where the attempt was already nothing, and nothing cannot contest anything, and the contest was over before it began because the contest had never existed because one of the participants was nothing.

And the future Godric had not even noticed. The shadow of an afterthought. A passing consideration. The future Godric had not turned his attention to the current Godric's attempt. He had not focused. He had not concentrated. He had not exerted. He had had a thought, a thought so trivial it barely qualified as a thought, and the metaphysical residue of that thought, the shadow it cast, was sufficient to completely stomp the current Godric's most powerful effort. The future Godric was not even aware that a contest had occurred. There was no contest. There was a thought, and there was nothing, and the nothing was the current Godric's best effort.

My hands were shaking. The shaking was familiar. I had been shaking, on and off, for forty-seven years. But this shaking was different. This shaking originated not in the chest but in the skull, in the deep, central structures of the brain, the parts that processed survival and threat and the fundamental assessment of whether the organism was safe. The assessment was returning a value that the organism had no category for. The organism was not unsafe. The organism was not threatened. The organism was in the presence of a being whose one, sole, only limitation, the single thing in all of existence that the being could not do, was something that the being could not do because a later version of himself, a version so powerful that the current version was nothing, had decided he could not do it. The limitation was not external. It was not imposed by an enemy. It was not a flaw in the system. It was a being being surpassed by himself, later, and the later self was so much more powerful that the earlier self's will was nothing relative to his.

And that was when the second realization hit me, the realization that made the first one look like a preface.

The limitation was not a weakness.

I had been told that Godric had one true weakness. I had been told that the Stasis Event was irrevocable, that the pre-Stasis mortal past could not be altered, that Godric could not save his family by changing history. And the word weakness had been used, and I had accepted the word, because the word was technically accurate. Godric could not do something. There was something he wanted to do, save his family, and he could not do it. That was a limitation. That was, by definition, a weakness.

But it was not a weakness.

It was a guarantee.

If the Stasis Event was created by Godric's future self, then the existence of the future self was guaranteed. The future Godric existed. He was not a possibility. He was not a potential outcome. He was not a maybe. He existed, in an era so distant that the year could not be written, and he was so powerful that the current Godric was nothing compared to him. The current Godric would become the future Godric. The amplification would continue. The superpower genesis would continue. The recursive, infinite, escalating growth that had been proceeding for a million years would proceed for a span of time so vast that a million years was a rounding error. The current Godric would grow, and grow, and grow, and the growing would never stop, and eventually, at some point in a future so distant it could not be measured, the current Godric would become the future Godric, the being whose afterthought's shadow could stomp the current Godric's best effort.

The limitation was a timeline. It was a confirmation. It was a receipt, stamped and signed by the omnipotence of the future self, certifying that the current Godric would survive, would grow, would amplify, would become something so incomprehensibly more powerful that his current self would be nothing. The limitation said: you cannot alter the past because your future self has sealed it. And the sealing of the past, the irrevocability of the Stasis Event, the absolute permanence of every mechanism it initiated, the amplification, the superpower genesis, the absolute concealment, the Weakness Paradox, the immune system, the passive shields, the sentient auras, all of it, every single mechanism, was irrevocable because its creator was the future Godric, and the future Godric was the most powerful being that would ever exist.

Which meant that none of Godric's power mechanics could be exploited. None of them could be denied. None of them could be reversed, overridden, or undone by anyone, anywhere, at any point in time, because the origin of those mechanics was a being so powerful that the combined will of the entire omniverse, including the current Godric, was nothing relative to his. The amplification could not be stopped because the future Godric had willed it to continue. The Weakness Paradox could not be exploited because the future Godric had willed it to be absolute. The absolute concealment could not be breached because the future Godric had willed it to be impenetrable. The immune system could not be bypassed because the future Godric had willed it to be inviolable. Every mechanism, every system, every passive defense, every recursive amplification, every apocalyptic superpower, was anchored not in the current Godric's will but in the future Godric's will, and the future Godric's will was the supreme, absolute, unsurpassable authority in all of existence.

The "weakness" was not a weakness. It was the strongest possible guarantee. It was a certificate of invincibility, signed by a being so powerful that the certificate itself was more powerful than anything that could possibly oppose it. Godric's one limitation was that he could not overpower his future self, and his future self was himself, later, and the later self was more powerful, which meant that every limitation imposed by the future self was, in effect, a limitation imposed by Godric on himself at a later, more powerful stage. It was not a weakness. It was a closed loop of escalating supremacy, a self-referential architecture of absolute power where every limitation was a guarantee and every guarantee was an escalation and every escalation was permanent and the permanent was forever.

I tried to find the weakness. I tried, with every ounce of my amplified intellect, to find the exploit, the loophole, the angle, the strategy, the approach that an enemy could use. There was nothing. There was nothing because the "weakness" was internal, temporal, and self-referential. It was Godric being surpassed by Godric. It was a limitation imposed by the being himself, at a later stage, and the later stage was guaranteed, and the guarantee meant that the limitation was permanent, and the permanent meant that the limitation was not a weakness but a feature, a structural property of an existence that was designed, by its own future, to be unsurpassable.

An enemy could not exploit it. An enemy could not reach the pre-Stasis timeline, because reaching it required overpowering the future Godric's will, and the future Godric's will was the supreme authority in existence. An enemy could not use the limitation to weaken Godric, because the limitation was not a reduction in power. It was a confirmation of power. It confirmed that Godric would grow, that the growth would continue, that the amplification would never stop, and that the being at the end of the growth would be so powerful that the current Godric was nothing. An enemy could not weaponize the limitation because the limitation was not a gap in Godric's armor. It was the armor itself, forged by a future version of the being it protected, anchored in a will so absolute that nothing in existence could contest it.

The "weakness" was, in the most literal, most technical, most horrifying sense, an additional strength. It was the ultimate strength. It was the strength that said: every power mechanism you see, every amplification, every superpower, every defense, every immunity, every aura, every shield, every recursive escalation, is not merely the product of the current Godric's omnipotence. It is the product of the future Godric's omnipotence. And the future Godric's omnipotence is so vast, so absolute, so incomprehensibly superior to everything else in existence, that the mechanisms it creates are not merely powerful. They are irrevocable. They are permanent. They are absolute. They cannot be undone, reversed, overridden, exploited, or denied by anyone, anywhere, at any point in time, because the being who created them is the most powerful being that will ever exist, and his will is the supreme, unsurpassable authority in all of creation.

"You are processing," Godric said. His voice was gentle. Observant. The voice of a man watching a child solve a math problem that the child is not quite ready for.

"I am processing," I confirmed.

"Take your time."

I did not take my time. My brain, forty-nine years of thousandfold daily multiplication, processed the information at a speed that would have made the greatest intellects in the omniverse look like frozen photographs. The processing was instantaneous and total, and the result was a realization so complete, so absolute, so soul-crushing that it settled into my chest like a stone made of compressed existential horror and stayed there, permanently, a weight I would carry for the rest of my mortal life.

"Why?" I said. "Why did the future you create the Stasis Event?"

Godric tilted his head. The firelight caught his beard. His expression was thoughtful, the expression of a man considering a question he has considered many times before and has not fully answered because the answer belongs to a version of himself he has not yet become.

"I do not know with certainty," he said. "I know what the Stasis Event does. I know what it has given me. I know that the future me chose to create it, chose to use Thorin's spell as the delivery point, chose to transform a mortal teleportation into the birth of an apex predator. But the motive, the specific reason why the future me decided that this was necessary, that I cannot tell you with certainty. I can speculate."

He paused. The fire crackled.

"Perhaps the future me, existing in an era where opposition has ceased entirely, where no being in the omniverse dares to form even the slightest thought of resistance, where the concept of challenge has been permanently erased from creation, looked back at his own history and decided that the origin story needed to be real. Not manufactured. Not orchestrated. The future me did not cause the Fall of Olympus. He did not kill Reina, Isabel, Hebe, or Thorin. Those deaths were genuine. The Fall was genuine. The grief was genuine. But perhaps the future me, looking back, saw that the grief, the loss, the mortal man broken by tragedy, was the foundation of everything he became. Perhaps the Stasis Event was not a correction. Perhaps it was a preservation. A way of ensuring that the origin remained intact, that the mortal man who lost everything became the god who gained everything, and that the transition was permanent and irrevocable and could not be undone by any version of himself, including the current one who was driven by fresh grief to try to erase it."

He looked at me. His eyes were warm. His expression held no pain. No regret. No lingering grief. Only the settled, comfortable acceptance of a man who has made peace with a limitation and found the peace to be, if anything, more satisfying than the absence of the limitation would have been.

"Or perhaps," he said, a small, amused smile playing at the corner of his mouth, "the future me simply wanted to make sure that I would always have something to talk about with my court wizards. A conversation piece. A mystery to reveal at the appropriate moment, to watch the expression on their faces when they realize that the most powerful being in the omniverse has a weakness that is not a weakness, a limitation that is not a limitation, and a past that cannot be changed because the future has already decided it will not be."

He chuckled. A warm, genuine, amused chuckle. The chuckle of a man who finds his own existence darkly funny.

"Are you amused by this?" I asked. My voice was barely audible.

"I am," Godric said. "I was not, initially. When I first discovered the limitation, when I first tried to save them and was crushed by the shadow of an afterthought, I was not amused. I was angry. I was grief-stricken. I was a man who had just discovered that omnipotence has a boundary, and the boundary was himself, later, and the later self had decided that the boundary would stand. But that was a long time ago, Kaedan. A very long time ago. I have had a million years to process it. And in that million years, I have come to find it genuinely, deeply amusing."

He leaned forward. His eyes were bright with the same enthusiasm I had seen when he described the pleasure of his amplification, the same genuine, unforced delight in the mechanics of his own existence.

"Think about it, Kaedan," he said. "I am the most powerful being in the omniverse. I can do anything. I can annihilate existence with a breath. I can overwrite reality with a twitch. I am omnipotent, omniscient, absolute. And the one thing I cannot do, the single, sole, only limitation on my existence, is that I cannot overpower a version of myself that has not yet arrived. My weakness is my own future. My limitation is my own growth. The thing I cannot defeat is the thing I will become. That is not a weakness, Kaedan. That is a promise. The most powerful promise in existence. The promise that I will continue. That the amplification will not stop. That the growth will not cease. That I will become more, and the more will become more, and the more-more will become more, forever, without end, without ceiling, without limit. My weakness is the guarantee of my own eternal escalation. How can I not be amused by that?"

He sat back. He steepled his fingers. He looked at me with the warm, patient, affectionate expression of a favorite uncle watching his nephew process a lesson that the nephew is not quite ready to absorb but will absorb eventually because the uncle has faith in him.

"You are not troubled by it," I said. It was not a question.

"No," Godric said. "I am not. I was, once. Briefly. Painfully. But the grief passed. The anger passed. The desire to change the past passed. And what remained was the understanding that the limitation is not a limitation. It is a feature. A structural property of an existence that is designed, by its own future, to be unsurpassable. I cannot save them by changing the past. I accepted that. I made peace with it. And the peace is genuine, Kaedan. I am not performing acceptance. I am not simulating peace. I am comfortable with this limitation the way I am comfortable with the chair I am sitting in. It is part of the furniture of my existence, and I have arranged myself around it, and the arrangement is pleasant."

I stared at him. The fire was dying. The embers were fading. The study was growing dark, and the darkness was appropriate, because the thing I was processing could not be processed in light. It required darkness. It required the absence of illumination, because illumination implies understanding, and the thing I was processing was the understanding that understanding itself was insufficient.

The current Godric, the man sitting in front of me, was the most powerful being in the omniverse. His power multiplied beyond layered infinities every instant. His stats, his superpowers, his immune system, his auras, his passive shields, his absolute concealment, his Weakness Paradox, all of it, every mechanism, every system, was irrevocable and absolute because the creator of those mechanisms was a future version of himself so powerful that the current Godric was nothing relative to him. The "weakness" was that the current Godric could not overpower the future Godric's will. And the future Godric's will was the supreme authority in existence. And the future Godric was the current Godric, later. And the later was guaranteed. And the guarantee meant that every mechanism, every system, every defense, every escalation, was anchored in a will that nothing in existence could contest.

There was no exploit. There was no angle. There was no strategy, no weapon, no coalition, no force, no being, no combination of beings, no convergence of powers, no alignment of circumstances, no possible arrangement of any factor in any reality in any timeline in any dimension in any abstract layer of existence that could exploit this "weakness," because the "weakness" was Godric being surpassed by Godric, and the surpassing was guaranteed, and the guarantee was permanent, and the permanent was anchored in the will of the most powerful being that would ever exist.

Opposing Godric was not merely impossible. It was structurally, categorically, ontologically invalid. The concept of opposition to Godric was a contradiction in terms, a structural impossibility, like the concept of a shadow without light or a sound without vibration. Opposition required two forces of comparable scale. There was no force comparable to Godric. There was no force comparable to the future Godric. There was no force comparable to the shadow of the afterthought of the future Godric. The current Godric's best effort to alter the past had been erased by the shadow of an afterthought, and the shadow of an afterthought was less than nothing relative to the future Godric, and the future Godric was less than nothing relative to whatever Godric would become after that, and the recursion continued forever, each iteration making the previous one look like nothing, and there was no ceiling, and there was no floor, and there was no end.

And Godric was comfortable with this. He was amused. He was at peace. He was sitting in a leather chair in my study, describing the most horrifying truth in existence with the tone of a man recounting a pleasant anecdote, and his eyes were warm, and his smile was genuine, and he was not performing. He was not simulating. He was a man who had made peace with a limitation that was not a limitation, and the peace was real, and the real peace was the most terrifying thing I had ever witnessed, because it meant that the apex predator of all creation was content. Not restless. Not hungry. Not searching. Content. Satisfied. Comfortable. The being whose power grew infinitely every instant, whose future was guaranteed to be so powerful that his current self was nothing, whose every defense was anchored in the will of the most powerful being that would ever exist, was sitting in a chair and smiling at me and telling me that he was fine.

"Kaedan," Godric said, his voice soft. "You are shaking."

"I am always shaking," I said. "I have been shaking for forty-seven years."
