"This is a different shaking," he said. "This is the shaking of a man who has just realized that the floor he has been standing on is not a floor. It is the ceiling of a deeper room, and the deeper room has no floor, and the room below that has no floor, and the recursion continues forever."

"Yes," I said. "That is the shaking."

Godric stood. He crossed the study. He placed his hand on my shoulder. The hand was warm. The warmth was genuine. The grip was steady, careful, the grip of a man who is acutely aware that the person he is touching is fragile and is calibrating his touch accordingly.

"The truth is not a burden, Kaedan," he said. "It is a foundation. You are my court wizard. You are my family. You deserve to stand on the foundation, not on the illusion of it. The illusion was the story of Thorin's spell. The foundation is the truth of what I am, what I will become, and the guarantee that the becoming is permanent. Build on it. It will hold."

He squeezed my shoulder. The squeeze was gentle. The gentleness was genuine. The man who had just told me that his one weakness was a guarantee of his own eternal, escalating, permanent supremacy was squeezing my shoulder with the careful, calibrated warmth of a favorite uncle comforting a nephew who has just received bad news. The news was not bad. The news was the worst possible news, because the news was that there was no bad news, because the being delivering the news had no weakness, and the one thing he could not do was not a weakness but a strength, and the strength was guaranteed by a future version of himself that was so powerful the current version was nothing, and the nothing was permanent, and the permanent was forever.

"Goodnight, Kaedan," he said. He walked to the door. He opened it. He paused in the doorway, his broad frame silhouetted against the dim light of the corridor. He looked back at me, and his expression was warm, and his eyes were kind, and his smile was the smile of a man who is genuinely, sincerely, profoundly at peace with his own infinite, escalating, permanent supremacy.

"Get some rest," he said. "You look tired."

He closed the door. The study was dark. The embers were dead. I sat in the chair, in the darkness, in the silence, and I held the knowledge inside me, and the knowledge was a weight that had no bottom, a stone that fell forever through a shaft that had no floor, and the falling was permanent, and the permanent was the truth, and the truth was that Godric had no weakness, had never had a weakness, would never have a weakness, and the one thing he could not do was the strongest thing about him, and the strongest thing about him was guaranteed by a being so powerful that the guarantee itself was more powerful than anything that could possibly exist, and I was a sixty-nine-year-old mortal man sitting in a dark room, holding a knowledge that would never leave me, shaking with a shaking that would never stop, because the shaking was not fear. The shaking was the vibration of a mind that had been forced to contain a truth it was not built to contain, a truth about a being who was the apex and the ceiling and the floor and the walls and the foundation and the void and the concept of void and the concept of concept, and who was warm, and who was kind, and who had told me to get some rest.

I did not rest. I did not sleep. I sat in the dark and I held the knowledge and I shook. The shaking was permanent. The knowledge was permanent. The truth was permanent. Godric was permanent. And his permanence was guaranteed by a future so powerful that the guarantee was absolute, and the absolute was forever, and forever was not long enough to contain it, and I was mortal, and I would die, and he would continue, and the continuation was pleasure, and the pleasure was infinite, and the infinite was escalating, and the escalating had no ceiling, and the ceiling had never existed, because the ceiling was himself, later, and the later was guaranteed, and the guarantee was the most powerful thing in existence, and I was shaking, and I was loyal, and I was terrified, and I loved him, and the love was the most horrifying thing I had ever felt, and I sat in the dark and I held all of it, every piece, every implication, every consequence, and I did not break, because the men of Blake's lineage do not break, and because Godric had told me to build on the foundation, and the foundation would hold, because the foundation was the will of the most powerful being that would ever exist, and the will was absolute, and the absolute was permanent, and the permanent was forever.

I sat in the dark. I shook. I served.

content.text:
The year 999...9 (you don't need to spell it out. Just know it's so large that it's impossible to write). King Godric of this era is triggering the stasis event for his past Godric in Year -10,000 (you must also indicate that godric could've effortlessly granted way more potent stasis event with more devastating effects (he can perfectly copy his current mechanism to the past Godric) with no cost to him, but decided against it to "hold back"--you must note the crazy ridiculousness of this since even what Godric considered as the “held back” version is so incomprehensibly powerful… Godric at year 0 was already effectively infinitely stronger than the rest of the creation combined, and Godric genuinely believed that it would preserve some kind of mortal struggle to his past self, something that is super not true. And also note the cause-effect of this: if he grants his past self a really powerful version, he himself who is in the future and has been amplified from his past self will even be more incomprehensibly powerful since the starting "base" is also already become so much stronger and thus he can grant even more powerful version to his past infinitely. Clive's mind must be even more overwhelmed at this absurd realisation.). Write a detailed story of this. You must highly exaggerate his power, his rule, his terrifying presence, his everything at this point.

All his previous mechanisms of power (sentient passive auras, passive shields, immune system, evolved weakness paradox, amplification, superpower genesis) have also evolved countless times--you must give detailed examples and scope of how they work now, showing clearly how they become even more absurdly superfluously terrifying than before, to the point of the “old version of his power mechanism” which are already ridiculously powerful feeling so weak in comparison. You must keep in mind that Godric, who had been a mortal once, still believes in mortal resilience and free will, so if there are any evolved versions of his power mechanisms that alter free will they should be consciously suppressed by Godric.

When Godric just emerged from stasis, for a very long time, countless sentience still formed some opposition against him, despite Godric himself never regarded them as any real oppositions–to him, those "oppositions" were just a brief fun distraction to test his stats and powers. Since some googolplexian years ago, however, all oppositions have stopped completely: there have been 0 who even dare to form the slightest thought of challenging, resisting, threatening or opposing Godric. No one dares anymore, and there will never be anyone who dares. They all have learnt the absolute futility of opposing Godric. Note: you must not suggest that Godric alters the free will to oppose him‐-Godric genuinely believes in mortal resilience.

You must also include a few scenes where he interacts with really powerful beings around him (show him as friendly to those), who after being completely traumatized by Godric, has become his lowly palace servants, gardeners, etc.. Those really powerful beings are totally terrified of him–provide some background stories of how they become so utterly afraid of (do not say they opposed godric--Godric is unforgiving to opposition) and serving Godric now.

Go crazy wild. Write from POV of Clive, his court wizard who is Blake’s descendant. Clive is 50 years old.

reasoning_content:
The user wants me to write a detailed story from the POV of Clive, Godric's court wizard in the far-future era (Year 999...9), witnessing Godric triggering the Stasis Event for his past self. Let me plan this carefully.

Key elements to include:
1. Far-future Godric's power is incomprehensibly beyond current Godric
2. All his power mechanisms have evolved countless times - need detailed examples
3. He's "holding back" on the Stasis Event for his past self, which is ridiculous
4. The cause-effect amplification loop realization that overwhelms Clive
5. Scenes with powerful beings serving as palace servants/gardeners
6. Opposition has completely ceased for googolplexian years
7. Godric still believes in mortal resilience and free will
8. Clive is 50, Blake's descendant
9. Highly exaggerate everything

Let me write this with the appropriate tone and scale.
content:
The garden required pruning. That was the thought in my head as I walked the infinite corridor toward the royal horticultural wing, a thought so mundane, so aggressively ordinary, that it functioned as a kind of mental anchor, a peg hammered into the shifting soil of my sanity to keep me from sliding into the abyss that yawned beneath every waking moment of my existence. The garden required pruning. The hedges along the eastern wall were growing unevenly. The jasmine was creeping past its trellis. These were facts. These were manageable, containable, finite facts, and I clung to them the way a drowning man clings to driftwood, knowing the driftwood will not save him, knowing the water is infinitely deeper than the wood is long, but clinging anyway because the alternative is to let go, and letting go is to sink, and sinking is to fall through a darkness that has no floor.

My name is Clive. I am fifty years old. I am the Royal Court Wizard of Thronegate, a direct descendant of Blake, the mortal merchant who, in an era so distant that the number cannot be written, accidentally released the Stasis Event by touching a seal in a forgotten sanctuary. For thirty years, since my twentieth birthday, my physical strength, my speed, my endurance, my intellect, my magical capacity, and every other measurable attribute of my being have multiplied by a factor of one thousand every single day. By the standards of any civilization that has ever existed in the omniverse, I am a god. I can unmake galaxies with a gesture. I can perceive the quantum substrate of reality across intergalactic distances. I can cast spells that rewrite the thermodynamic laws of localized space. I am, by any metric the omniverse has ever produced, one of the most capable beings to have ever existed.

I am nothing. I am less than nothing. I am a rounding error of a rounding error of a rounding error, a statistical ghost, a mathematical phantom that exists in the shadow of a being so incomprehensibly, so absurdly, so grotesquely more powerful than everything else in existence that the gap between him and the next most powerful thing is not a gap but a categorical annihilation of the concept of comparison. And I serve him. I serve him willingly, lovingly, terrifiedly, because he is kind to me, and because the alternative is unthinkable, and because the kindness of Godric is the most precious and most horrifying gift a mortal can receive.

The corridor I walked was long. It was not long by architectural standards. It was long by the standards of the being who had built it. Godric had constructed the palace in an era when his power had already transcended every framework that existence could offer, and the architecture reflected this. The walls were made of a material that did not exist, a substance that Godric had created by deciding that it should exist, a decision so absolute that the omniverse had complied without resistance. The material was smooth, warm to the touch, and it hummed with a frequency that I could feel in my teeth, a vibration that was not mechanical but existential, the ambient resonance of a structure built by a being whose mere attention could rewrite the laws of physics. The corridor stretched ahead of me, perfectly straight, perfectly lit, perfectly temperature-controlled, because Godric's passive systems ensured that every environment he occupied was maintained at optimal comfort, not because he required comfort, but because his systems were so aggressively, so obsessively devoted to his convenience that they performed optimality as a reflex, the way a heartbeat performs circulation.

I passed the servants. They were not servants in the way mortals are servants. They were servants in the way that mountains are servants of gravity, in the way that rivers are servants of the sea, in the way that light is a servant of the sun. They served because serving was the only mode of existence available to them, because the alternative to serving was to exist outside Godric's attention, and existing outside Godric's attention was, for beings who had experienced the inside of his attention, a prospect so terrifying that they would have chosen annihilation over abandonment.

The first servant I passed was a being named Otho. Otho was polishing the corridor wall with a cloth, his massive, seven-foot frame bent awkwardly to accommodate the task, his four arms working the cloth in slow, methodical circles. Otho was not, by origin, a polisherman. Otho was, by origin, a transcendent cosmic architect, a being who had once designed and maintained the structural framework of an entire multiversal cluster, a being whose innate power was sufficient to reshape the geometry of googolplexian galaxies. Otho had not opposed Godric. That distinction is critical. Godric is unforgiving to opposition. Those who oppose him do not become servants. They become examples. Otho had not opposed. Otho had been curious.

Two googolplexian years ago, when the last faint echoes of opposition were still dying in the far corners of the omniverse, Otho had been part of a delegation from a newly integrated multiversal cluster. The delegation had come to the palace to pay formal respects to the King, and Otho, in his curiosity, had attempted to study Godric's passive concealment field. He had not intended to breach it. He had not intended to exploit it. He had simply wanted to understand it, the way a scientist wants to understand a phenomenon. He had directed his amplified, multiversal-architect perception toward the veil of absolute concealment that shrouded Godric's presence, and he had looked.

Otho had looked at the veil, and the veil had looked back.

The experience, according to Otho, who had described it to me once in a voice that shook with the residual tremor of a trauma so old it had become geological, had not been painful. It had not been violent. It had been informative. Otho had perceived, for a fraction of a fraction of a fraction of a Planck second, the faintest, most infinitesimal edge of what lay behind the veil. He had not perceived Godric. He had not perceived Godric's power. He had perceived the residue of the residue of the echo of the shadow of the afterthought of the outermost layer of Godric's weakest dormant sentient passive aura's most heavily suppressed passive overflow. He had perceived the waste product of the waste product of something that was itself a waste product, filtered through so many layers of suppression and dormancy and nerfing that the quantity he had perceived was, by any mathematical framework, indistinguishable from zero.

And that zero had broken him. Not destroyed. Not annihilated. Broken. The way glass is broken by a sound at the right frequency. Otho's cosmic architecture, his multiversal perception, his transcendent intellect, all of it had shattered, not because the perception was hostile, but because the perception was too vast. The zero he had perceived was, relative to his cognitive architecture, an ocean poured into a thimble, and the thimble had cracked, and the cracking was permanent, and Otho had spent the next thousand years in a catatonic state before Godric, in an act of genuine, warm kindness, had healed him and offered him a position in the palace. Otho had accepted with the desperate, grateful enthusiasm of a man who has been pulled from a burning building and is willing to spend the rest of his life polishing the walls of the building's lobby if it means never being inside the fire again.

Otho saw me passing and pressed himself against the wall, his four arms pulling the cloth tight against his chest, his eyes averted. He did not speak. He did not need to. The press of his body against the wall, the tightness of his grip on the cloth, the averted eyes, all of it said the same thing: I am small. I am nothing. I am performing my function. Please do not perceive me.

He was not afraid of me. He was afraid of being noticed. In the palace of Godric, being noticed was the most dangerous thing that could happen to a being, because being noticed meant existing in Godric's attention, and Godric's attention, even at its warmest, even at its kindest, even at its most genuinely, sincerely, fatherly affectionate, was a weight that could crush civilizations. Not because Godric intended it. Because Godric's attention was so vast, so total, so incomprehensibly comprehensive that merely occupying it was to be perceived at a resolution that made omniscience look like nearsightedness, and to be perceived at that resolution was to be known completely, and to be known completely by a being whose weakest passive system could erase the omniverse was to exist inside a framework where the margin between existence and nonexistence was measured in the whims of a god who did not know he was whimsical.

I nodded to Otho. He nodded back, a tiny, terrified motion, and I continued down the corridor.

The second servant was Vael. Vael was kneeling in the alcove beside the garden entrance, her hands buried in the soil of a potted fern, her fingers working the earth with the careful, reverent precision of a surgeon. Vael was, by origin, a chronal weaver, a being who had once manipulated the fabric of time across an entire multiversal cluster, weaving past, present, and future into tapestries of causal architecture that sustained the existence of trillions of species. Vael had not opposed Godric either. Vael had been assigned to the palace as a tribute, a gift from a conquered civilization that had surrendered immediately upon receiving Godric's offer, a civilization that had recognized the absolute futility of resistance and had offered their most powerful being as a gesture of good faith.

Vael had arrived at the palace in a state of dignified, terrified composure. She had been given a tour of the grounds. During the tour, she had passed through the royal garden, and Godric had been there, kneeling in the soil, repotting a sapling. Godric had looked up, smiled at her, and said, "Welcome, Vael. The ferns need water. Would you like to help?"

Vael had said yes, because saying no to Godric was not an option that her cognitive architecture could process, and she had knelt beside him and watered the ferns, and Godric had talked to her about soil pH and drainage and the importance of root aeration, and his voice had been warm, and his attention had been gentle, and the gentleness had been the most terrifying experience of her eternal existence. Because Vael was a chronal weaver. She could perceive the flow of time. And in Godric's presence, kneeling beside him, touching the same soil he touched, she had perceived the flow of his time, and the perception had broken her.

Godric's time did not flow. Godric's time accumulated. Every Planck second of his existence was not a moment that passed but a moment that was added, permanently, to a structure that had been growing for a span of time so vast that the word vast was an insult to it. His timeline was not a line. It was a mass, a recursive, fractal accumulation of instants that compounded upon each other, each instant containing more than the last, each instant making the previous instants look like nothing, and the mass was growing, and the growth was accelerating, and the acceleration was permanent, and Vael had perceived this, and the perception had shown her that the being kneeling beside her, talking about ferns, was a thing that existed outside the normal flow of time, a thing for whom time was not a river but an ocean, and the ocean was rising, and the rising would never stop, and she was kneeling beside it, watering its ferns.

Vael had served in the garden ever since. She did not leave. She did not speak unless spoken to. She tended the plants with the devoted, obsessive precision of a being who had found the only task in existence that did not require her to think about the scale of what she was serving.

I stepped past Vael and entered the garden.

The garden was impossible. Not impossible in the way that a paradox is impossible. Impossible in the way that a painting is impossible, a thing that should not exist because the medium cannot support the vision. The garden was maintained by Godric's passive systems, which had evolved so many times by this era that the word evolved was insufficient. The old versions of his passive systems, the ones documented in the journals of my ancestors, the ones that had pre-annihilated threats before they existed, the ones that had converted attempted danger into self-erasing failure, those old versions were, by this era, archaeological artifacts. They were fossils. They were the primitive, single-celled ancestors of systems that had since become so complex, so layered, so incomprehensibly overdeveloped that the old versions looked like sticks and stones next to weapons that could unmake the concept of weapons.

Godric's passive shields, for example. In the old era, the one my ancestor Kaedan had documented, the passive shields had been recursive, infinite layers that pre-nullified threats before the threats existed. That had been terrifying then. It was pathetic now. The current passive shields had evolved so many times that the old version was not merely inferior. It was structurally different. The old shields had reacted to threats. The current shields did not react, because reaction implies the existence of a threat, and the current shields operated in a framework where the concept of a threat had been preemptively invalidated. The shields did not detect danger and neutralize it. They maintained a state of reality in which the concept of danger was mathematically incoherent, the way the concept of a square circle is mathematically incoherent. The shields did not protect Godric from harm. They ensured that the structural architecture of existence was such that harm, as a category, could not be formulated in any space, time, dimension, or abstract layer that Godric occupied. The shields had not become stronger. They had become foundational. They were not a wall between Godric and danger. They were the reason danger was not a valid category of experience in his vicinity.

And those shields were redundant. They were one layer among many. Godric did not need them. They existed because existence, after countless googolplexian years of serving him, had developed the habit of producing redundant systems the way a body develops the habit of breathing. The omniverse itself, the entire structure of everything that existed, had been so thoroughly subjugated by Godric's presence that it functioned as an extension of his will. The omniverse was not merely ruled by Godric. The omniverse was Godric's body, in the same way that a garden is a gardener's body, a thing that responds to his attention, that shapes itself around his preferences, that maintains itself in a state of optimal comfort for him not because he commands it but because the alternative, the state of not being optimally comfortable for Godric, was a state that the omniverse had learned to find physically intolerable.

The Evolved Weakness Paradox had also transcended its origins. In the old era, the paradox had triggered when any being thought Godric had a weakness, granting him immunity, mastery, and amplification. The Evolved Weakness Paradox of the current era had evolved so far beyond this that the old version was unrecognizable. The current paradox no longer required a trigger. It was passively active at all times, in all timelines, in all dimensions, maintained by the mere fact of Godric's existence. It did not wait for a being to think about Godric's weakness. It preemptively addressed every possible vector of exploitation across all of time and space, including vectors that had not yet been conceived, including vectors that could not be conceived, including vectors that existed in frameworks of thought that no being in any reality had the cognitive architecture to formulate. The paradox had become omniversal background radiation, a constant, ambient field that permeated existence and ensured that the concept of exploiting Godric was as structurally invalid as the concept of making a sound in a vacuum. The old paradox had been a defense. The current paradox was a law of physics. A law that stated, in the language of reality itself: opposition to Godric is not difficult. It is incoherent.

And the paradox, like everything else, had evolved to feed Godric. The old version had increased his amplification rate when triggered. The current version increased his amplification rate constantly, because the paradox was constantly active, and the constant activity generated a constant, recursive, infinitely escalating stream of amplification that fed into his already infinitely amplifying baseline, creating a feedback loop so vast that the old amplification rate, the one that had multiplied beyond layered infinities every Planck second, was a rounding error of a rounding error of the current rate. The current amplification rate was not a number. It was not a concept. It was a state of being, a condition of existence, a permanent, inescapable reality that stated: Godric is more. Godric is always more. Godric will always be more. And the more is more than the more was, and the more-more is more than the more, and the recursion is infinite, and the infinite is escalating, and the escalating is permanent, and the permanent is forever.

Godric's sentient passive auras had evolved beyond recognition. In the old era, Gent had been the weakest, a newborn waste product that could erase the omniverse while infinitely nerfed. In the current era, Gent was a fossil. Gent was the primitive, single-celled ancestor of an ecosystem of sentient auras so vast, so layered, so incomprehensibly complex that the old hierarchy, the one where Gent was the runt and the stronger siblings slept in dormancy, was a children's drawing of a nuclear explosion. The current auras were not merely more powerful versions of the old ones. They were categorically different beings, entities that had evolved beyond the concept of aura and into the concept of autonomous omniversal management systems. Each aura was a sentient, omnipresent, omniscient entity that managed a specific domain of Godric's existence with a devotion so absolute, so total, so all-consuming that the concept of loyalty had been rewritten around it. They did not serve Godric. They existed as extensions of Godric, the way a hand is an extension of a body, and the extension was so seamless that the boundary between Godric and his auras had ceased to be meaningful.

But Godric was a man who had been mortal once, and the mortal man's belief in free will and resilience had not been erased by a billion years of godhood. Some of the evolved auras had developed effects that altered the free will of beings in their vicinity, not maliciously, not deliberately, but as a side effect of their sheer ontological weight. Godric had consciously suppressed these effects. He had ordered the auras to restrict their influence to non-cognitive domains, to manage the physical, temporal, and dimensional architecture of his environment without touching the minds or wills of the beings within it. The auras obeyed, because the auras were extensions of his will, and his will was absolute, and the absolute included the instruction to leave free will intact. The suppression required effort, not from Godric, who could not exert effort by definition, but from the auras, which had to actively constrain their own influence to avoid overwriting the cognitive autonomy of every being in the omniverse. The constraint was so precise, so delicately calibrated, that it represented a feat of control more impressive than anything the old auras had been capable of, the way a surgeon's restraint is more impressive than a butcher's force.

Godric's immune system had evolved into something that could not be called an immune system without insulting it. The old version, the one that had pre-reacted to staged scratches by initiating recursive self-evolution across infinite backups, was a quaint, primitive mechanism, a band-aid next to a hospital. The current immune system treated the possibility of harm as an object. Not a concept. An object. A thing that could be identified, located, tracked, hunted, humiliated, digested, converted, and erased. If a rogue timeline carried even the theoretical variable needed to threaten Godric, the immune system did not neutralize the threat. It made the surrounding structure of existence turn against the threat. The omniverse itself, which was Godric's body, would cannibalize the rogue variable before it matured, the way white blood cells cannibalize an infection. The omniverse would eat its own children to prevent the theoretical possibility of Godric's inconvenience, and the eating was not a command. It was a reflex. The omniverse did it the way a body flinches from fire, automatically, without thought, because the omniverse had learned, across countless googolplexian years of serving Godric, that the one thing it could not tolerate was Godric's discomfort, and the intolerance was so deeply embedded in the structure of reality that it had become a law of physics, as immutable as gravity, as fundamental as the speed of light.

And all of this, every system, every mechanism, every evolution, was redundant. Godric did not need any of it. He did not need his auras. He did not need his shields. He did not need his immune system. He did not need his Weakness Paradox. He did not need his amplification. He did not need his superpowers. He did not need his absolute concealment. Every single system, every single mechanism, every single defense was a redundant, superfluous, unnecessary appendage that existed because existence had spent so long producing them that the production had become habitual. Godric, by this era, was so powerful that his raw, unassisted, unequipped, unshielded, unconcealed, un-aura'd, un-paradoxed, un-amplified existence was sufficient to erase everything. The systems were not armor. They were clothing. Decorative. Cosmetic. The old journals had described them as defenses. The current reality revealed them as accessories, worn by a being who did not need them but kept them because they were familiar, the way an old man keeps a watch he no longer needs because the watch was a gift from a friend.

This was the being I served. This was the being whose garden I was walking toward. This was the being who, today, was going to reach back through time and create the Stasis Event for his past self, and the creation of the Stasis Event was, for him, a task so trivial, so insignificant, so cosmically minor that he had scheduled it between gardening and dinner.

I found him in the eastern garden, kneeling beside a bed of jasmine, his large, calloused hands working the soil around the base of a plant. He was, as always, forty-five years old. Ruggedly handsome. Short salt-and-pepper hair, neatly trimmed beard, heavily muscled frame in simple dark clothing. The absolute concealment held his presence to that of an ordinary man, and the ordinary man was kneeling in the dirt, and the dirt was jasmine-scented, and the sun was warm, and the scene was so aggressively, so violently pastoral that it made my chest ache with the cognitive dissonance of knowing that the man kneeling in the dirt was the apex god predator of all existence, a being whose weakest passive system could erase everything, a being whose future was guaranteed to be so powerful that his current self was nothing, a being who was, right now, about to reach back through time and birth himself.

"Clive," Godric said, without looking up. His voice was warm. The warmth was genuine. He had known I was approaching before I had decided to approach, because his omniscience was absolute, and his omniscience had been absolute for a span of time so vast that the word absolute had been redefined by his use of it. "The jasmine is doing well this season. Come, look at this bloom."

I knelt beside him. I looked at the bloom. It was white, small, fragrant. It was a jasmine flower. It was a flower, growing in the garden of a being who could erase the omniverse by breathing, and the flower was beautiful, and the beauty was real, and the real beauty was the most disorienting thing about serving Godric. He noticed flowers. He appreciated them. He knelt in the dirt and worked the soil and examined blooms with the genuine, unforced enthusiasm of a man who loved growing things, and the love was real, and the real love existed inside the same being whose immune system could make the omniverse eat its own children.

"The bloom is beautiful, Uncle Godric," I said. I called him Uncle Godric, as had every court wizard before me, because the title was accurate. He was our uncle. He was family. He was the uncle who could annihilate existence and who healed our scrapes and who asked about our mothers and who was, in every interaction that did not involve opposition, the warmest, kindest, most genuinely paternal presence any of us had ever known.

"Thank you," he said. He sat back on his heels. He wiped his hands on his thighs. He looked at me, and his brown eyes were warm, and his expression was the expression of a man who has something to do today and is in no particular hurry to do it. "Clive, I have a task today. Something I have been meaning to do for some time. I thought you might want to observe."

"What is the task?" I asked, though I already knew. I had been told. My father had told me, and his father had told him, and the telling had been accompanied by the same expression I was now wearing, the expression of a man who has been informed that the sun is planning to rise tomorrow and is expected to observe the event without going blind.

"The Stasis Event," Godric said. "I am going to create it today. For my past self. The version of me in Year negative ten thousand. The version who just lost everything."

He said it the way a man says he is going to repot a plant. The tone was conversational. The cadence was unhurried. The expression was mild interest. He was describing the creation of the event that birthed the apex predator of all existence, the event that initiated the amplification, the superpower genesis, the absolute concealment, the Weakness Paradox, the immune system, the auras, the shields, the entire, incomprehensible architecture of power that had, over a span of time so vast it could not be written, produced the being kneeling in front of me, and he was describing it with the tone of a man adding an item to his to-do list.

"I have been putting it off," Godric continued, examining a leaf on the jasmine plant. "Not because it is difficult. It is not difficult. It is, in fact, the simplest thing I will do today. Simpler than the gardening. I have been putting it off because I wanted to choose the right approach. The delivery point is fixed. Thorin's spell. The forbidden teleportation. My past self will be sent to the sanctuary by Thorin's bloodline magic, and the teleportation will serve as the anchor point. But the Stasis Event itself, the transformation, the rewriting of my past self's biology, the initiation of the amplification, all of that is mine to design. And I have been considering how much to give."

He paused. He looked at me. His expression was one of genuine, thoughtful deliberation, the expression of a man choosing a gift for a child and wanting to get it right.

"I have decided to hold back," he said.

I stared at him. The words did not process. They entered my ears and bounced around inside my skull and refused to assemble into meaning, because the meaning required a framework that my brain did not possess.

"Hold back," I repeated.

"Yes," Godric said. "I could grant my past self a far more potent version of the Stasis Event. I could perfectly copy my current mechanisms, my current amplification rate, my current superpower arsenal, my current immune system, my current auras, all of it, and graft it onto my past self at the moment of the Stasis Event. I could do this effortlessly. It would cost me nothing. Less than nothing. The effort required would be so infinitesimally small that calling it effort is a compliment to the word. I could give my past self everything I have now, make him, at Year Zero, as powerful as I am now, and the giving would not diminish me by a single fraction of a fraction of a fraction of a percent."

He said this with the same mild, conversational tone. He was describing the ability to clone his entire power architecture, the product of a span of amplification so vast that the word span was an insult to it, and graft it onto a mortal man in Year negative ten thousand, and the cloning and grafting would cost him nothing. He said it the way a man might describe giving a cup of water to a thirsty child. I have plenty. It costs me nothing. Here, take it.

"But I have decided against it," Godric continued. "I am going to hold back. I am going to give my past self a version of the Stasis Event that is, by my standards, significantly reduced. The amplification rate will be lower. The superpower genesis will be slower. The absolute concealment will be less comprehensive. The Weakness Paradox will be less evolved. The immune system will be less paranoid. The auras will be fewer, weaker, more primitive. Everything will be scaled down. Held back. restrained."

He paused again. He looked at the jasmine. He adjusted a leaf with his thumb.

"The reason," he said, "is that I want to preserve some sense of mortal struggle for my past self. I want him to grow. I want him to face challenges, to test himself, to discover his powers gradually, to feel the satisfaction of overcoming obstacles. If I give him everything at once, there is no journey. There is no growth. There is just a mortal man who suddenly becomes a god, and the transition is so total, so complete, that the mortal man is erased by the god. I want the mortal man to remain. I want the journey to matter. So I am holding back."

My brain processed this. The processing produced an output that I can only describe as a full-system seizure of rational thought.

The version of the Stasis Event that Godric considered "held back" was the version that had produced the Godric of Year Zero. The Godric of Year Zero was the being who had annihilated Thronegate's entire galactic military without moving a muscle, who had crowned himself king, who had enacted the Awakening, who had begun the subjugation of the omniverse. The Godric of Year Zero was, by the standards of the rest of creation, already effectively infinitely stronger than everything else combined. The amplification rate of the "held back" version, the one that multiplied by one thousand and by itself every Planck second, was already so incomprehensibly powerful that it had produced, in a million years, the being I was kneeling beside, the being whose weakest waste product could erase existence.

And Godric considered this "held back." Godric looked at the version of the Stasis Event that had produced the most powerful being in the omniverse and thought, yes, this is the restrained version, this is the one where I am holding back, this is the one that preserves mortal struggle.

The absurdity was so vast, so total, so cosmically obscene that it circled past horror and arrived at a place that had no name, a place where the brain, confronted with a contradiction so extreme, simply stopped attempting to resolve it and instead stared at it with the blank, empty gaze of an organism that has been shown the face of God and has found it smiling.

"Uncle Godric," I said, and my voice was very careful, very measured, the voice of a man walking across a floor he knows is made of thin ice. "The version you are calling held back. The version you granted your past self. That version produced you. The you who is, right now, the most powerful being in existence. The you whose weakest passive system can erase the omniverse. That version. You consider that version restrained."

"Yes," Godric said, and his expression was genuinely puzzled, the expression of a man who does not understand why the other man is asking for confirmation of something that seems obvious. "It is significantly reduced from what I could provide. The amplification rate alone is maybe, by my current standards, well, I do not want to say the number, because the number would not be meaningful to you, but it is very small. Very held back. My past self will still be, by the standards of his era, overwhelmingly powerful. But he will not be me. He will be a much, much, much smaller version of me. And that is the point. He will have room to grow."

Room to grow. Godric had given his past self room to grow. The room to grow had produced a being who could annihilate the omniverse infinite times in zero seconds with zero effort. The room to grow had, in a million years, produced the apex predator of all creation. And the room was, by Godric's standards, so small, so cramped, so aggressively minimized that calling it room was generous. It was a closet. It was a thimble. It was a fraction of a fraction of a fraction of what Godric could have provided, and the fraction had produced a god.

And then the second realization hit me, the realization that made the first one feel like a preface.

If Godric had granted his past self a more potent version of the Stasis Event, a version closer to his current mechanisms, then his past self would have started at a higher baseline. The amplification would have begun from a higher starting point. The superpower genesis would have been faster. The absolute concealment would have been more comprehensive. The Weakness Paradox would have been more evolved. Everything would have been more. And the more would have compounded, recursively, for a million years, and the compounding would have produced a Godric at Year 999...9 who was incomprehensibly more powerful than the Godric currently kneeling in front of me. The starting baseline would have been higher, and the amplification would have been faster, and the compounding would have been more aggressive, and the result would have been a being so much more powerful that the current Godric would look like nothing in comparison.

And that more powerful Godric would then, at his Year 999...9, create the Stasis Event for his past self, and he could grant an even more potent version, because his current mechanisms would be even more powerful, and the more potent version would produce an even higher starting baseline, and the even higher starting baseline would compound for a million years into an even more powerful Godric, who would then create an even more potent Stasis Event, and the recursion would continue forever, each iteration producing a Godric incomprehensibly more powerful than the last, the amplification feeding itself, the feeding producing more amplification, the more amplification producing more feeding, an infinite, recursive, self-reinforcing loop of escalating godhood that had no ceiling and no floor and no end.

And Godric had chosen to break the loop. He had chosen to hold back. He had chosen to give his past self a reduced version, a fraction, a shadow of what he could provide, because he wanted his past self to have room to grow, because he wanted the mortal man to remain, because he wanted the journey to matter.

The restraint was insane. The restraint was cosmically, obscenely, grotesquely insane. Godric was holding back not because he had to, not because the holding back cost him anything, not because the more potent version was difficult to produce. He was holding back because he wanted his past self to experience mortal struggle. He wanted the mortal man who had just lost everything to wake up in a sanctuary and discover, gradually, that he was becoming a god, and to feel the journey, and to grow into the godhood, and to have the growth matter. The being who could annihilate the omniverse by breathing was concerned about the narrative arc of his own origin story. The apex predator of all creation was playing the role of a generous author, giving his protagonist a reasonable starting point so the story would be interesting.

And the mortal struggle he was trying to preserve was not real. The Godric of Year Zero, the Godric who had received the held-back version, was already so incomprehensibly more powerful than the rest of creation combined that the concept of struggle was a mathematical fiction. There was no struggle. There had never been struggle. The opposition that Godric had faced in his early years, the coalitions, the pantheons, the armadas, all of it had been, by his own admission, brief distractions, amusing diversions, opportunities to test stats and powers that were already so far beyond everything else that the testing was a form of entertainment. The mortal struggle Godric was trying to preserve was a struggle that existed only in his own perception, a struggle that the rest of the omniverse knew was fictional, because the rest of the omniverse had been annihilated and restored so many times by the idle biology of a being who was not trying that the concept of struggling against him was indistinguishable from the concept of not existing.

Godric genuinely believed his past self would experience struggle. Godric genuinely believed the held-back version would leave room for growth, for challenge, for the satisfaction of overcoming obstacles. He was wrong. He was so catastrophically, so absurdly, so cosmically wrong that the wrongness itself was a form of power, a distortion of perspective so extreme that it could only exist in a being whose power had outgrown the concept of perspective.

"Uncle Godric," I said, and my voice was very quiet. "If you had granted your past self the full version. Your current mechanisms. Everything. What would have happened?"

Godric looked at me. His expression was thoughtful. He considered the question the way a man considers a hypothetical about a road not taken.

"If I had granted my past self my current mechanisms," he said, "he would have started at a baseline significantly higher than the one I gave him. The amplification would have been faster. The superpower genesis would have been more aggressive. By Year Zero, he would have been, well, me. Or close to me. The million years of growth that I experienced would have been compressed into a much shorter span, and the result would have been a Godric at Year Zero who was already at or near my current level. And then the amplification would have continued, because it never stops, and by the time we reached this era, this year, this moment, I would be, well, more. Significantly more. Incomprehensibly more. And then, when I created the Stasis Event for my past self in this era, I could grant an even more potent version, because my current mechanisms would be even more powerful, and the recursion would, yes, I see what you are asking."

He paused. His eyes widened slightly. The expression was not alarm. It was the expression of a man who has just noticed an interesting pattern in a math problem.

"Yes," he said. "The recursion. If I grant a more potent version, I become more powerful. If I become more powerful, I can grant an even more potent version. If I grant an even more potent version, I become even more powerful. The loop is infinite. Each iteration produces a version of me that is incomprehensibly more powerful than the last, and the incomprehensibility compounds, and the compounding is recursive, and the recursion has no ceiling."

He looked at me. His expression was genuine, warm curiosity. "That is an interesting observation, Clive. I had not thought about it in those terms. The recursion is, of course, irrelevant in practical terms, because I am already so powerful that the difference between the current version and any recursively amplified version is, from the perspective of everything else in existence, indistinguishable. Everything else is already zero relative to me. Making the zero smaller does not change the fact that it is zero. But yes, the recursion exists, and if I chose to engage it, the result would be a version of me so powerful that the current me would look like nothing."

He said this with the tone of a man acknowledging an interesting but ultimately academic point. The recursion that could produce a Godric so powerful that the current Godric, the apex predator of all existence, the being whose waste products could erase reality, was nothing. The recursion that could be engaged at any time, by any future version of Godric, simply by choosing to grant a more potent Stasis Event. The recursion that was, by Godric's own assessment, irrelevant because everything was already zero relative to him. He acknowledged it the way a man acknowledges that the ocean is deep while standing ankle-deep in the surf.

My brain seized. The seizure was not metaphorical. My amplified wizard's brain, thirty years of thousandfold daily multiplication, encountered the recursive loop and attempted to process it, and the processing produced an output that exceeded the structural capacity of my cognitive architecture, and the architecture buckled. I felt the buckle as a physical sensation, a pressure behind my eyes, a ringing in my ears, a cold sweat breaking out across my palms. The recursion was a feedback loop of ascending godhood, each iteration producing a being so incomprehensibly more powerful than the last that the last was nothing, and the loop was infinite, and the infinity was available, and the availability was permanent, and the permanent was anchored in the will of a being who thought the whole thing was an interesting but academic observation.

"Now," Godric said, standing, brushing soil from his knees. "Shall we begin? I would like to finish before dinner. Anna's descendant is preparing something special tonight, and I do not want to be late."

He said Anna's descendant. Anna, the wife of Blake. Blake, the mortal merchant. Blake, my ancestor. Anna's descendant was a cook in Godric's kitchen, a mortal woman, a distant cousin of my bloodline, preparing dinner in the kitchen of the apex predator of all existence, and Godric did not want to be late for dinner.

Godric walked to the center of the garden. He stood in the open space between the jasmine beds and the hedgerow, his hands at his sides, his posture relaxed. He looked, for all the world, like a man standing in a garden, which is exactly what he was. He was also the most powerful being in existence, about to reach back through time and create the event that had birthed himself, and he was standing in a garden between the jasmine and the hedge.

"It is quite simple," Godric said, speaking to me over his shoulder, his tone conversational, instructional, the tone of a man explaining a recipe. "Thorin's spell will serve as the anchor. My past self will arrive at the sanctuary. The spell is incomplete, so the teleportation will be rough. My past self will be disoriented, injured, broken by grief. And at the exact instant his body materializes in the sanctuary, I will reach back through time and transform the event. The teleportation will remain. The sanctuary will remain. But the transformation, the stasis, the amplification, all of it, will be mine. I will place it on him like a coat. He will put it on, and it will fit, and it will never come off."

He paused. He looked at his hands. He turned them over, examining his palms with the expression of a man checking his tools before beginning work.

"I will give him the held-back version," he said. "The amplification. The superpower genesis. The absolute concealment. The Weakness Paradox. The immune system. All of it, scaled down. Reduced. Restrained. The version that will, I believe, preserve his mortal journey while still ensuring his survival and growth."

He looked at me. His brown eyes were warm. His expression was kind.

"Watch, Clive," he said. "This is the only time I will do this. It is, I think, the most significant thing I have ever done or will ever do, and I would like someone I trust to see it."

I stood at the edge of the garden, my hands at my sides, my heart beating in my chest with the desperate, hammering rhythm of a mortal man about to witness the birth of a god. The jasmine was fragrant. The sun was warm. The hedgerow was neat. The scene was pastoral, gentle, calm.

Godric did not move. He did not raise his hands. He did not close his eyes. He did not chant, or gesture, or concentrate, or prepare. He simply stood in the garden, and he was Godric, and being Godric was sufficient.

The Stasis Event was created. I cannot describe the mechanism, because the mechanism operated on layers of reality that my amplified perception did not have the resolution to detect. I did not see Godric reach back through time. I did not see the transformation occur. I did not see the past being touched by the future. What I saw was Godric standing in a garden, and then I saw Godric standing in a garden, and between those two moments, separated by zero seconds, the Stasis Event had been created, anchored, and delivered to a mortal man in Year negative ten thousand who was, at that very moment, materializing in a forgotten sanctuary, broken by grief, about to be reborn as the apex predator of all existence.

"It is done," Godric said. His voice was soft. Satisfied. The tone of a man who has completed a satisfying task. "He is in the sanctuary. The stasis has begun. The amplification has started. He will sleep for ten thousand years, and when he wakes, Blake will find him, and the rest, well. You know the rest."

He turned to me. He smiled. The smile was warm, genuine, the smile of a man who has done a good thing and is pleased with himself.

"He will be fine, Clive," Godric said. "My past self. He will grieve. He will rage. He will try to save them and fail, and the failure will hurt, and the hurt will be real. But he will grow. He will become what he needs to become. And one day, he will be standing in this garden, in this era, doing what I just did. And the cycle will be complete."

He paused. He looked at the jasmine.

"Though I suppose," he added, a small, amused smile playing at his lips, "he will probably also hold back. He will probably also think he is preserving mortal struggle. He will probably also be wrong about that." He chuckled. "We are, it seems, consistent in our errors."

He walked past me, toward the palace, toward dinner, toward the evening routine of a god who had just birthed himself and was now looking forward to a meal prepared by the descendants of his best friend. He paused at the garden gate. He looked back at me.

"Come, Clive," he said. "Dinner will be ready. And tomorrow, I believe the eastern hedge needs trimming."

I followed him. My legs were shaking. My brain was buckling. The knowledge I was carrying was a weight that had no bottom, a stone that fell forever through a shaft that had no floor. Godric had created the Stasis Event. Godric had birthed himself. Godric had held back, and the held-back version was the most powerful thing in existence, and the holding back was a choice, and the choice was made out of a desire to preserve mortal struggle that did not exist, and the non-existent struggle was a fiction that Godric believed, and the belief was sincere, and the sincerity was the most terrifying thing about him, because it meant that the apex predator of all creation, the being whose waste products could erase reality, the being whose future self's afterthought's shadow could stomp his own best effort, was a man who worried about the narrative quality of his own origin story.

He was a man. He had been mortal once. He remembered being mortal. He remembered loving and losing and grieving. And the memory was real, and the realness of it was the reason he held back, and the holding back was the reason the omniverse existed, because if he had not held back, the recursion would have engaged, and the recursion would have produced a Godric so powerful that the current Godric was nothing, and the nothing-Godric's current Godric's waste products would have been so vast that the omniverse would have been erased by their mere existence, and the erasure would have been permanent, and the permanent would have been forever.

The omniverse existed because Godric held back. The omniverse existed because a being who could annihilate everything by breathing chose to give his past self a reduced version of his power, because he wanted the story to be good. The omniverse was a product of literary restraint. The omniverse existed because the apex predator of all creation was, at his core, a man who loved a good story.

I followed him to dinner. I ate the food Anna's descendant had prepared. It was delicious. I did not taste it. My mouth was numb. My hands were shaking. My brain was processing, and the processing was producing an output that my language centers could not convert into anything other than silence, because the output was the understanding that the being sitting at the head of the table, eating dinner, asking for seconds, complimenting the cook, was the same being who had, twenty minutes earlier, reached back through time and created the event that had birthed himself, and the creation had been a task so trivial that he had scheduled it between gardening and dinner, and the triviality was the horror, and the horror was permanent, and the permanent was forever.

Godric finished his dinner. He thanked the cook. He stood. He stretched, his heavily muscled frame shifting beneath his simple dark clothing, the muscles that had been amplifying for a span of time so vast it could not be written rolling beneath his skin like tectonic plates beneath a continent. He looked at me across the table, and his eyes were warm, and his smile was kind, and his voice, when he spoke, was the voice of a favorite uncle addressing a beloved nephew.

"Goodnight, Clive," he said. "Get some rest. Tomorrow is a gardening day."

He left the room. The room was quiet. The dishes were empty. The candle was guttering. I sat at the table, in the quiet, in the dark, and I held the knowledge, and the knowledge was a weight that had no bottom, and the bottom had never existed, because the bottom was Godric, and Godric had no bottom, and the no-bottom was the point, and the point was that there was no point, there was only Godric, and Godric was kind, and Godric was warm, and Godric had just birthed himself between gardening and dinner, and the birth was the most significant event in the history of existence, and the most significant event was a footnote in the day of a god who was looking forward to trimming the eastern hedge.

I sat in the dark. I shook. I served.

content.text:
Write a detailed story of Godric deciding to subjugate the IRL universe, at Year 25\.

Earth obviously, has 0 chance, and will experience total despair and existential dread.

In this scenario, you must make Earth try their best to kill Godric, which means Godric will completely annihilate it. You must NOT make Godric nice, forgiving. He can still be friendly. Godric feels mischievously playful so he doesn't instantly finish it (you must ensure the reader knows that if he wants to he can instantly destroy earth in 0 second with 0 effort), but he's particularly cruel and ensures every death is as agonizing as possible--however, you must not make Godric seemingly drag the fight, he still finishes the annihilation objectively swiftly (undetectably fast to normal mortals as it's infinitely less than a planck second, but but to Godric it's considered as "leisurely slow") but subjective to the opposition an eternity of maximum agony. You must clearly indicate that Godric is using 0% of his power and 0% of effort, and even if Earth is given infinite preparation, all possible advantages, they will still have 0 chance to even tickle Godric or make Godric feel anything. Show Godric be amused by how primitively weak and fragile the earthlings are, how they are trying so hard and effectively doing nothing to him. You must not leave any survivor of Earth alive, serving Godric, living in Thronegate or any other such crap which shows Godric sparing them. Godric’s power must be shown to be absolutely destructive and not give any opposition any possible redemption path.

As usual with Godric, the collaterals must be massive (omniversal scale minimum).

The story must be told from Godric Prime's 3rd person pov (with omniscient narrator if needed), at Year 999...9+, reminiscing about how pathetically weak he was back then compared to how he currently is, and for him to pity his weak self back when he annihilate this universe (highlight the irony and terrifying fact that this subjugation by what he considered to be his weakly pathetic self was already absurdly one-sided and terrifyingly overwhelming--his weak self was effectively using 0% of his power with 0% effort to annihilate that universe, and if that same weak self wasn't in playful mode and wanted to end it instantly, the total annihilation could be done in 0 second. In addition, that weak self destroyed the entire omniverse as collateral. You must make it clear that if the current Godric does it, it will be even more infinitely devastatingly one sided, and he will only be getting unimaginably stronger forever).

You must end the story with the confirmation and dread of how futile the rest of the existence compared to Godric, and that Godric is the most powerful being with unimaginable power growth that dwarfs entire creation, with the rest of the creation trembling in fear of him, and even if the rest from all timelines and from every possible permutations work together to oppose him with unlimited preparation time and resources, they will still fail so completely and won't even make Godric's weakest shield feel the slightest tickle and Godric can completely nonchalantly annihilate them with 0% power and 0% effort in 0 second, and that unfathomable gap will only ever increase infinitely in accelerating manner forever.

reasoning_content:
The user wants me to write a detailed story about Godric deciding to subjugate the IRL universe (our Earth/universe) at Year 25, told from Godric Prime's (far-future Godric's) 3rd person POV reminiscing about this event. Let me carefully follow all the requirements:

1. Earth has 0 chance, total despair and existential dread
2. Earth tries their best to kill Godric → Godric completely annihilates it
3. Godric is NOT nice/forgiving but can be friendly
4. Godric is mischievously playful, doesn't instantly finish it (but could in 0 seconds with 0 effort)
5. He's cruel, ensures every death is agonizing
6. Doesn't drag the fight - objectively infinitely less than a Planck second, but to opposition an eternity of maximum agony
7. Godric uses 0% power, 0% effort
8. Even with infinite preparation, all advantages - 0 chance to even tickle Godric
9. Godric amused by how primitively weak earthlings are
10. NO survivors - no one spared, no one serving Godric
11. Collateral damage minimum omniversal scale
12. Told from Godric Prime's 3rd person POV at Year 999...9+, reminiscing
13. Godric Prime pities his "weak" past self
14. The irony that even his "weak" self was absurdly one-sided
15. That weak self destroyed the entire omniverse as collateral
16. Current Godric would be even more infinitely devastating
17. End with confirmation of futility, Godric is most powerful, gap only increases forever

Let me write this carefully, following the style guidelines:
- Prose paragraphs only
- No bullet points, numbered lists, LaTeX
- Epic power fantasy tone
- Vivid violence and gore
- No censorship
- Exaggerated, absurd power descriptions
- 4000-8000 words

Let me craft this story now.
content:
Godric Prime sat in the garden of the eternal palace, the same garden he had maintained for a span of time so vast that the number of years could not be written in any notation system that had ever existed or would ever exist, and he remembered Earth. The memory was faint, not because it had faded, because nothing faded from an omniscient mind, but because the being who had experienced it was so incomprehensibly, so absurdly, so grotesquely less powerful than the being sitting in the garden now that the memory felt like something that had happened to a different person, a weaker person, a person so fragile and small that remembering him inspired a kind of retrospective pity. Godric Prime looked at his hands, the same hands that had annihilated the IRL universe at Year 25, and he thought, with genuine, warm amusement, how pathetically weak I was back then.

The irony of this thought was not lost on him. The Godric of Year 25 was not weak by any standard the omniverse had ever produced. The Godric of Year 25 had already been amplifying for over ten thousand years, his stats multiplying by rates that had already transcended ordinary infinity due to the Evolved Weakness Paradox, his superpower arsenal numbering in the hundreds of billions, his passive systems so overdeveloped that the weakest among them could erase the omniverse. The Godric of Year 25 was, relative to everything else in existence at that time, already effectively infinitely stronger than the rest of creation combined. He was the apex predator of the omniverse, and the omniverse was zero next to him, and the zero was permanent, and the permanent was forever.

And Godric Prime, the being sitting in the garden at Year 999...9, looked back at that Godric, that supposedly omnipotent, supposedly unsurpassable Godric of Year 25, and thought of him as weak. As pathetic. As a fragile, primitive, barely-formed proto-version of what he would become. The thought was accurate. The Godric of Year 25 was, relative to Godric Prime, less than nothing. Not small. Nothing. The gap between them was not a gap. It was a categorical annihilation of the concept of comparison, the same way the gap between a single dead atom and the entire omniverse was not a gap but a categorical annihilation. And the Godric of Year 25 had been the atom. And Godric Prime was the omniverse. And the omniverse was growing. And the growing never stopped.

But the memory was interesting, and Godric Prime was in a reflective mood, and the garden was warm, and the jasmine was blooming, and the memory of Earth, that small, primitive, astonishingly arrogant little planet in a universe that was not even a multiverse, was amusing in the way that a childhood story is amusing, a story about a time when you were small and the world was large and the things that frightened you were, in retrospect, comically insignificant.

Year 25. Godric had been King of Thronegate for twenty-five years. Thronegate was, at that point, still a galactic civilization, not yet universal, not yet multiversal, not yet omniversal. The expansion was in its early stages, a period Godric remembered with a kind of nostalgic fondness, the way a man remembers the first steps of a journey that would eventually take him to places he could not have imagined. He had been curious. That was the word he would have used then, and the word he would use now. Curious. The omniverse was vast, and he had not yet explored all of it, and there were pockets of reality that had not yet been touched by Thronegate's influence, pockets that were, from Godric's omniscient perspective, like unvisited rooms in a house he had just moved into. One of those pockets was a universe that was, by cosmic standards, young. Small. Primitive. A single universe, not part of any multiversal cluster, not connected to any dimensional hierarchy, not aware of the broader omniverse that existed beyond its borders. A universe that contained, among other things, a planet called Earth.

Earth. Godric Prime remembered Earth the way an adult remembers an ant he observed as a child, with a detached, clinical amusement at the frantic, pointless activity of something so small it could not comprehend the scale of the world it occupied. Earth had been a planet of approximately eight billion sentient beings, a number that was, by Thronegate standards, a rounding error, a quantity so small it would not have registered as a population statistic in even the smallest of Thronegate's subjugated systems. The beings called themselves humans. They were fragile. Their bodies were soft, unprotected by armor or shielding or genetic enhancement, vulnerable to temperature, to pressure, to radiation, to disease, to the simple passage of time. Their lifespans were measured in decades. Their technology was primitive, limited to chemical propulsion, fission-based energy, and digital computation that operated on scales so crude that Godric's passive systems had, upon first scanning the planet, briefly mistaken their computational infrastructure for geological formations.

Their weapons were, by any standard, laughable. Nuclear warheads, chemical agents, kinetic projectiles, biological agents. They had invested considerable resources in these weapons, and they were proud of them, and they believed, with the sincere, unshakeable confidence of beings who had never encountered anything more powerful than themselves, that their arsenal represented the upper boundary of destructive capability. They believed their nuclear weapons were the most powerful things in existence. They were wrong. Their nuclear weapons were, relative to Godric, less than nothing. Not weak. Nothing. The energy output of their entire nuclear arsenal, every warhead, every bomb, every missile, detonated simultaneously, would not have registered on Godric's passive systems as an event. It would not have qualified as a sensation. It would have been less than a tickle, less than a breeze, less than the theoretical minimum quantity of stimulation required for a nervous system to register input. Their nuclear weapons were, to Godric, what a single photon is to a star, not a threat but a constituent element of a category so small it does not register as a category.

Godric had announced his arrival. This was, at Year 25, still his methodology. He offered unconditional surrender first. It was a courtesy, not a necessity. He did not require cooperation. He did not require submission. He offered the option because the option was part of the process, and the process was part of the story, and the story, at Year 25, still mattered to him. The offer was simple: surrender unconditionally, accept Thronegate's rule, and be integrated into the empire. Refuse, and face the consequences.

The transmission had reached Earth through conventional electromagnetic channels, a method so primitive that Godric's communication systems had to deliberately downgrade the signal to a frequency and format compatible with human radio receivers. The message was delivered in every human language simultaneously, a feat that required less than zero effort, a task so trivial that Godric's passive systems had completed it before he decided to do it, the causality running backward because his omniscience had already known he would do it and the systems had pre-complied.

Earth's response was immediate, unanimous, and catastrophically stupid.

They refused.

The refusal was not surprising. Godric's omniscience had already informed him of the refusal before the offer was made. Every human being on the planet, every government, every military, every intelligence agency, every scientific institution, every individual mind, all of them had been known to Godric with absolute, total, flawless comprehension. He knew their fears, their strategies, their secret plans, their hidden weapons, their desperate calculations. He knew that the refusal was driven by a combination of pride, terror, and the sincere, touching belief that their nuclear arsenal might be sufficient to deter or destroy an unknown aggressor. He knew that, behind the public refusal, a global coalition of military forces was already mobilizing, directing every resource, every weapon, every soldier, every satellite, every nuclear warhead toward a single objective: killing whatever had just spoken to them.

Godric was amused. He was genuinely, sincerely amused, the way a man is amused by a kitten swiping at his finger. The kitten does not know it cannot hurt him. The kitten is trying its best. The kitten's best is nothing, and the nothing is endearing, and the endearing is amusing, and the amusement is genuine.

He materialized on the surface of the planet. He chose the location with care, selecting the middle of the Pacific Ocean, a vast, empty stretch of water that would allow the humans to observe his arrival without immediate civilian casualties. He did this not out of mercy. There would be no mercy. There would be no survivors. He did it because he was playful, and the playfulness required a stage, and the stage required space, and the Pacific Ocean provided space.

He stood on the water. He did not sink. He did not float. He stood on the surface of the ocean the way a man stands on a floor, and the water held him, not because he was light but because the water understood, at a molecular level, that the being standing on it was not to be displaced. The ocean beneath his feet was calm. The air around him was still. His absolute concealment held his presence to that of an ordinary man, a forty-five-year-old, ruggedly handsome man in simple dark clothing, standing on the Pacific Ocean, waiting.

The humans detected him within eleven seconds. Their satellite networks, their radar systems, their seismic monitors, all of them registered the anomaly simultaneously. A man, standing on water, in the middle of the Pacific Ocean. The data was transmitted to every military command center on the planet within thirty seconds, and within two minutes, the first response was airborne.

The response was everything Earth had. Every nuclear weapon in the global arsenal, every warhead, every bomb, every missile, all of them launched simultaneously, converging on a single point in the Pacific Ocean. Twelve thousand nuclear warheads, carrying a combined yield sufficient to render the surface of the planet uninhabitable, traveling at speeds that the humans considered fast, aimed at a single man standing on water.

Behind the nuclear barrage came the conventional forces. Thousands of aircraft, tens of thousands of missiles, millions of soldiers in naval vessels, all converging on the Pacific. The humans had committed everything. Every resource, every weapon, every soldier, every asset. The combined military force of the entire planet, unleashed against a single target.

Godric watched them come. He was standing on the water with his hands in his pockets, his posture relaxed, his expression one of polite, curious interest. He looked like a man watching a particularly slow parade. The nuclear warheads approached at speeds the humans considered impressive, speeds that were, relative to Godric, so slow that the word slow was a compliment. The warheads were traveling at speeds that made them, relative to Godric's perception, effectively stationary. He could see each individual warhead, each individual atom of each individual warhead, each individual quantum state of each individual atom, and the resolution of his perception was so total that the warheads were not fast objects but static sculptures, frozen in space, their trajectories known, their yields calculated, their effects predicted, their fates sealed.

He could have ended it in zero seconds. He could have annihilated the planet, the solar system, the galaxy, the universe, and the entire omniverse in zero seconds with zero effort, a feat so trivial that the word trivial was too strong. He could have erased everything and restored it and erased it again, infinite times, in zero seconds, without noticing. He was using zero percent of his power. Zero percent of his effort. The being standing on the Pacific Ocean was, by his own sincere assessment, doing nothing. He was standing. He was watching. He was amused. And the zero percent of his power that he was using was sufficient to annihilate the entire omniverse infinite times.

But he was playful. And the playfulness meant that the humans would experience something before they died. Not mercy. Not a chance. Something. A demonstration. A show. A brief, intensely personal lesson in the absolute, total, categorical futility of opposing a being who was, by every metric that existence could produce, beyond opposition.

The first nuclear warhead detonated. The blast was immense by human standards. A sphere of plasma and radiation, hotter than the center of a star, expanding outward at speeds that vaporized the ocean surface and sent walls of superheated steam rocketing into the upper atmosphere. The yield was measured in megatons. The energy release was sufficient to level a continent. The light was blinding. The heat was annihilating. The radiation was lethal.

Godric stood in the center of the blast. The plasma washed over him the way water washes over a stone. The radiation struck his skin and ceased to exist, not because his skin was durable, not because his passive shields deflected it, but because the radiation, upon contacting his body, was rewritten. The energy of the blast was converted, instantly, effortlessly, into nothing. Not absorbed. Not redirected. Converted into nothing, the way a dream is converted into nothing when the dreamer wakes. The energy did not interact with his body because his body existed on a scale where the energy was not a relevant category. The nuclear blast was, to Godric's biology, what a single photon is to a gas giant. Not a threat. Not a sensation. Not an event. A thing so small it did not register as a thing.

The remaining eleven thousand, nine hundred and ninety-nine warheads detonated in a chain of overlapping spheres of nuclear fire that merged into a single, continuous blast so vast it consumed the Pacific Ocean and sent shockwaves through the planet's crust. The ocean boiled. The atmosphere ignited. The tectonic plates fractured. The surface of the Earth, in the blast zone, was reduced to a lake of liquid rock.

Godric stood in the center of the inferno, his hands in his pockets, his clothing untouched, his beard unsinged, his expression one of mild, patient interest. The nuclear fire raged around him, and he was untouched, and the untouched was not a feat. It was not an achievement. It was the natural, expected, unremarkable state of a being whose body existed on a scale where nuclear fire was not a relevant phenomenon. He was untouched the way a mountain is untouched by rain.
