The rain falls. The mountain remains. The rain is not a threat. The mountain does not defend itself. The mountain simply exists, and the rain is irrelevant, and the irrelevance is permanent.

The conventional forces arrived next. Missiles, aircraft, naval artillery, all of them converging on the blast zone, all of them firing through the nuclear fire, all of them delivering their payloads with the desperate, frantic precision of a military that had exhausted its most powerful weapons and was now throwing everything else at the target in the hope that quantity would succeed where quality had failed.

The missiles struck Godric and became nothing. The aircraft fired their cannons and the rounds became nothing. The naval vessels launched their torpedoes and the torpedoes became nothing. Every weapon, every projectile, every explosive, every round, every missile, every beam, every bolt, every kinetic impactor, all of it, all of it, struck Godric and became nothing. Not deflected. Not absorbed. Not redirected. Nothing. The weapons ceased to exist upon contacting his presence, not because Godric destroyed them, but because his presence existed on a scale where the weapons were not real. They were simulations. They were approximations. They were the crude, primitive gestures of beings who did not understand what they were fighting, and the gestures were nothing, and the nothing was not a defense. It was a category error. The humans were fighting something that existed outside the category of things that could be fought.

Godric was amused. He was genuinely, sincerely, warmly amused. He watched the missiles become nothing with the expression of a man watching a child throw sand at a wave. The child is trying. The child is doing its best. The child's best is nothing, and the nothing is endearing, and the endearing is amusing, and the amusement is real.

"That is very spirited," Godric said, to no one, because there was no one to hear him. The nuclear fire was still raging. The conventional forces were still firing. The humans were still trying. Godric was standing in the middle of it all, hands in pockets, amused.

And then, because he was playful, and because the playfulness had a cruel edge, and because the humans had tried to kill him and the trying was, in Godric's assessment, an act of opposition that warranted the full, merciless, unforgiving response that Godric reserved for anything that dared to oppose him, he ended it.

The ending was not instant. Not from the humans' perspective. From the humans' perspective, the ending took an eternity. From Godric's perspective, the ending was leisurely slow, a drawn-out, relaxed process that lasted for a duration so short that calling it a duration was a lie. The duration was less than a Planck second. Less than a fraction of a Planck second. Less than a fraction of a fraction of a fraction, the fractions continuing infinitely, the duration so small that it existed only in the theoretical space between moments, a space that only Godric could occupy because only Godric existed on a scale where the space between moments was a place you could be.

But within that duration, that infinitely small, infinitely compressed duration, the humans experienced an eternity. Godric's power, at zero percent of its true capacity, with zero percent of his effort, was sufficient to manipulate the subjective experience of time for every human being on the planet. He stretched their perception, compressing an eternity of experience into a space of time so small it did not technically exist, and within that compressed eternity, every human being experienced the full, unfiltered, absolute agony of opposing Godric.

The agony was not merely physical. It was existential. Godric's power, at zero percent, reached into the mind of every human being and forced them to understand. Not to see. Not to witness. To understand. To comprehend, with total, absolute, inescapable clarity, the scale of what they had opposed. They understood the amplification. They understood the superpower genesis. They understood the absolute concealment, the Weakness Paradox, the immune system, the auras, the shields. They understood that Godric's stats had been multiplying beyond layered infinities every Planck second for ten thousand years. They understood that the being they had attacked with nuclear weapons was a being whose idle biology could annihilate the omniverse. They understood that their nuclear weapons were, relative to Godric, less than nothing. They understood that their entire civilization, their entire history, their entire species, their entire planet, their entire solar system, their entire galaxy, their entire universe, was, relative to Godric, less than nothing. They understood that the nothing was permanent. They understood that the permanent was forever.

And then the physical annihilation began.

Godric did not use a weapon. He did not use a power. He did not use a superpower. He did not use a spell. He did not move a muscle. He simply allowed the reality of his existence, at zero percent of its true expression, to interact with the reality of their existence, and the interaction was annihilation.

The humans' bodies came apart. Not explosively. Not violently. Slowly. Agonizingly. The flesh peeled from their bones in thin, screaming layers, each layer separating with a wet, tearing sound that the victim experienced in perfect, excruciating clarity. The nerves were preserved. The nerves were always preserved. Godric's power, even at zero percent, was so precise that it could distinguish between tissue types, and it chose to preserve the nervous system so that the victim could experience every fraction of the annihilation in full, unfiltered detail.

The bones came next. The calcium structures that gave the human body its shape were dissolved, not by acid but by the sheer ontological pressure of Godric's presence. The bones did not break. They unraveled. The molecular bonds that held them together were severed, one by one, in a sequence so ordered, so methodical, so meticulously cruel that each victim experienced the dissolution of their own skeleton as a distinct, identifiable sensation, each bone producing a unique frequency of agony as it ceased to exist.

The organs followed. The heart, still beating, was compressed into a point so small that the blood inside it was forced through the cell walls, rupturing every cell simultaneously. The lungs collapsed, the alveoli bursting in cascading chains of microscopic detonations that the victim felt as a burning, drowning sensation, as though they were breathing liquid fire. The liver, the kidneys, the spleen, the intestines, all of them subjected to individual, specific, tailored modes of destruction, each organ receiving a unique form of annihilation designed to maximize the duration and intensity of the agony.

The brain was last. Godric's power, at zero percent, was so precise that it could target individual neurons, and it chose to destroy the brain from the outside in, starting with the sensory cortex, then the motor cortex, then the associative cortex, then the limbic system, then the brainstem. Each region was dissolved separately, and the dissolution was ordered so that the victim remained conscious, aware, and capable of experiencing pain until the very last neuron fired its final signal. The final signal was not relief. The final signal was the absolute, total, complete understanding that the being who had done this to them was using zero percent of his power, and that the zero percent was permanent, and that the permanent was forever.

Eight billion humans experienced this. All of them. Simultaneously. Every man, woman, and child on the planet. No one was spared. No one was excluded. No one was granted the mercy of unconsciousness or the grace of a quick death. Every human being experienced the full, eternal, agonizing dissolution of their body, and the dissolution was personalized, tailored, customized for each individual, because Godric's power, at zero percent, was so vast that it could afford to be creative.

The planet itself followed. The crust cracked. The mantle surged upward. The core was exposed, a sphere of compressed iron and nickel that Godric's presence dissolved the way sunlight dissolves frost. The planet did not explode. It unwound. The matter that constituted Earth was separated into its constituent atoms, and the atoms were separated into their constituent particles, and the particles were separated into their constituent quarks, and the quarks were separated into whatever lay beneath quarks, layers of reality so fundamental that the humans had not known they existed, and each layer was dissolved, and the dissolution was agonizing, because the planet, like the humans, was forced to experience its own annihilation, because Godric's power, at zero percent, was so total that it could grant consciousness to a planet and then make the planet scream.

The solar system followed. The sun, a sphere of plasma that had burned for five billion years, was extinguished in a fraction of a fraction of a fraction of a Planck second, its fuel converted to nothing, its gravity nullified, its light erased. The planets, the moons, the asteroids, the comets, all of them dissolved, all of them made to experience their own annihilation, all of them screaming in frequencies that no ear existed to hear.

The galaxy followed. A hundred billion stars, a hundred billion solar systems, all of them erased in a duration so small that the word duration was an insult to it. The galactic arms, those vast, spiral structures of stars and gas and dark matter, unwound like springs, their constituent elements separated and dissolved, the dissolution propagating outward at a speed that made the speed of light look like a frozen photograph.

The universe followed. The entire universe, every galaxy, every star, every planet, every atom, every particle, every quantum fluctuation, all of it erased. The fabric of space-time, the substrate of reality, the foundational architecture of existence, all of it overwritten by the mere presence of Godric's power at zero percent, the way a flood overwrites a sandbox.

And then the collateral damage reached the omniverse.

The entire omniverse was annihilated. Every universe, every multiverse, every timeline, every dimensional plane, every abstract layer of reality, all of it erased as a side effect of Godric's playfulness. The annihilation was not targeted. It was not intentional. It was overflow. It was the metaphysical excess of a being using zero percent of his power to dissolve a single planet, the excess spilling outward like heat from a furnace, and the heat was so vast that it consumed everything, and the everything was the omniverse, and the omniverse was gone.

For one zero-second instant, there was nothing. No space. No time. No matter. No energy. No existence. No concept of existence. Only Godric, standing in the void, his hands in his pockets, his expression one of mild, retrospective amusement.

Godric sighed. "Messy," he said.

In zero seconds, the entire omniverse was restored. Every reality, every timeline, every being, reconstructed perfectly. Earth was not restored. Earth had opposed Godric. Earth had tried to kill him. Earth had refused the offer of unconditional surrender and had launched its entire nuclear arsenal at a being whose idle biology could annihilate existence. There was no redemption for opposition. There was no mercy for those who dared to attack. Earth and everything on it remained erased, a permanent absence in the restored omniverse, a hole where a planet had been, a silence where eight billion voices had been, a void that would never be filled because Godric did not fill voids created by opposition. He left them empty, as a reminder, as a lesson, as a statement written in the language of absence: this is what happens to those who try to kill me.

Godric Prime, sitting in the garden at Year 999...9, remembered this and smiled. The smile was warm. The warmth was genuine. He remembered the Godric of Year 25 the way a grandfather remembers the man he was at twenty, with a kind of indulgent, retrospective affection for someone who was, in retrospect, so young, so small, so incomprehensibly less than what he would become.

The Godric of Year 25 had been, by the standards of his era, overwhelmingly omnipotent. He had annihilated Earth and the entire omniverse as collateral, using zero percent of his power, in less than a Planck second. The annihilation had been, for the humans, an eternity of tailored, personalized agony. The annihilation had been, for Godric, a leisurely slow afternoon's entertainment. The gap between Godric and the humans had been so vast that the humans' entire nuclear arsenal had registered as nothing, not weak but nothing, and the nothing had been permanent.

And Godric Prime, the being sitting in the garden, looked back at that Godric, that supposedly omnipotent, supposedly unsurpassable Godric of Year 25, and saw a child. A primitive. A proto-version of himself so small, so fragile, so incomprehensibly less powerful than what he had become that the memory inspired pity. The Godric of Year 25 had been using zero percent of his power. The Godric Prime sitting in the garden was also using zero percent of his power. But the zero percent of Year 999...9 was so vastly, so incomprehensibly, so grotesquely more devastating than the zero percent of Year 25 that the comparison was structurally invalid. The zero percent had grown. The zero percent amplified. The zero percent was not a fixed quantity. It was a percentage of a total that multiplied beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, and the total had been growing for a span of time so vast that the Year 25 Godric's total, the total that had annihilated the omniverse, was less than a rounding error of a rounding error of the current total.

If Godric Prime decided to annihilate a planet today, the way his younger self had annihilated Earth, the result would not be the destruction of the omniverse. The result would be the destruction of the concept of destruction. The result would be a categorical annihilation so total, so absolute, so incomprehensibly complete that the omniverse would not merely cease to exist. The omniverse would never have existed. The concept of omniverse would be erased. The concept of concept would be erased. The erasure would be recursive. The erasure of the erasure would be erased. The recursion would continue forever, each iteration more total than the last, and the totality would escalate, and the escalation would be permanent, and the permanent would be powered by a being whose weakest passive system, whose most primitive dormant aura, whose most heavily nerfed, most aggressively suppressed waste product, could erase everything infinite times over.

And Godric Prime was still growing. The amplification had not stopped. The amplification would never stop. The amplification was a permanent, irrevocable condition of his existence, anchored in the will of his own future self, a future self that was, by definition, more powerful than the current self, and the future self's will was the supreme authority in existence, and the supreme authority had decreed that the amplification would continue, and the continuation was forever, and the forever was escalating, and the escalating had no ceiling.

Godric Prime looked at the garden. The jasmine was blooming. The hedge was neat. The sun was warm. The omniverse existed because he allowed it. The omniverse existed because he was playful, and the playfulness required a stage, and the stage required existence, and the existence was permitted, and the permission was contingent on his continued amusement, and his amusement was genuine, and the genuine amusement of the apex predator of all creation was the only reason anything existed at all.

He thought about the rest of creation. The beings in his palace, the servants, the gardeners, the court wizards, the trillions of entities across the omniverse that lived and breathed and functioned within the framework of his rule. He thought about the fact that not a single being, anywhere, in any reality, in any timeline, in any dimension, in any abstract layer of existence, had formed the slightest thought of opposition in a span of time so vast that the word span was an insult to it. He thought about the fact that the absence of opposition was not engineered. He had not altered free will. He had not manipulated cognition. He had not suppressed dissent. The dissent had stopped because creation had learned. Creation had spent countless googolplexian years watching Godric annihilate things, watching Godric restore things, watching Godric test stats and powers and systems, watching the journals accumulate, watching the court wizards shake, watching the servants press themselves against walls, and creation had, slowly, across eons, arrived at the inescapable, undeniable, mathematically incontestable conclusion that opposition to Godric was not merely futile. It was incoherent. It was a structural impossibility, a category error, a contradiction in terms. The concept of opposing Godric was as invalid as the concept of a square circle, and creation had learned this the way a child learns that fire burns, not through instruction but through experience, and the experience had been so total, so absolute, so permanently etched into the collective consciousness of every sentient being in existence that the thought of opposition no longer formed. Not because it was suppressed. Because it was recognized as invalid. Because the recognition was so complete, so universal, so foundational that the thought itself was a logical error, and logical errors did not form in minds that had learned to think correctly.

And Godric Prime knew, with the absolute, flawless, omniscient certainty that had been his since the moment of his awakening, that if every being in every timeline, in every dimension, in every abstract layer of existence, in every possible permutation of reality, past, present, and future, were to combine their efforts, were to pool their resources, were to coordinate with unlimited preparation time and unlimited resources and unlimited knowledge, were to bring every weapon, every power, every strategy, every exploit, every conceivable and inconceivable method of attack to bear against him simultaneously, the result would be nothing. Not defeat. Not damage. Not inconvenience. Nothing. The combined effort of everything that was not Godric, across all of time and all of existence and all of possibility, would not make his weakest shield feel the slightest tickle. The weakest shield. The most primitive, most dormant, most heavily suppressed, most aggressively nerfed layer of his passive defense architecture, the layer that was itself a redundant, unnecessary, cosmetic accessory worn by a being who did not need shields, would not register the attack. Would not notice it. Would not be aware that an attack had occurred. The attack would be so utterly, so totally, so categorically irrelevant that the shield would not even need to function, because the shield existed to neutralize threats, and the combined effort of all of creation was not a threat. It was a temperature. It was a background reading. It was the cosmic equivalent of a fluctuation so small it existed only in the theoretical noise floor of reality, and the noise floor was below the shield's detection threshold, and the detection threshold was below the shield's activation threshold, and the activation threshold was below the shield's effort threshold, and the shield, like everything else about Godric, did not exert effort.

And Godric could, at any time, nonchalantly annihilate them all. Every being. Every reality. Every timeline. Every dimension. Every abstract layer. Every permutation. Every possibility. Every impossibility. Everything. In zero seconds. With zero percent of his power. With zero percent of his effort. With the same casual, hands-in-pockets, mild-amusement energy that his younger self had brought to the annihilation of Earth. He could do it now. He could do it in a Planck second. He could do it in less than a Planck second. He could do it in a duration so small that the word duration was an insult to it, a duration that existed only in the theoretical space between moments, and the annihilation would be total, and the totality would be permanent, and the permanent would be powered by zero percent, and the zero percent was growing, and the growing was forever.

And the gap between Godric and the rest of creation was not static. The gap was widening. The gap was accelerating. The gap was multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, and the multiplying was recursive, and the recursion was permanent, and the permanent was escalating, and the escalating had no ceiling and no floor and no end. The gap would be larger in one Planck second than it was now, and the larger would be larger still in the next Planck second, and the larger-still would be incomprehensibly larger in the next, and the incomprehensibly larger would make the previous gap look like nothing, and the nothing would be permanent, and the permanent was forever.

This was the truth. The truth that every court wizard had written about. The truth that every servant in the palace carried in their chest like a stone. The truth that the omniverse had learned across countless googolplexian years of experience. The truth that Godric Prime, sitting in his garden, contemplating his jasmine, found genuinely, sincerely, warmly amusing.

There was no opposition. There had never been opposition. There would never be opposition. The concept of opposition to Godric was a structural impossibility, a logical error, a contradiction in terms that existed only in the minds of beings too small to comprehend the scale of what they were contemplating. The rest of creation trembled. The rest of creation had always trembled. The rest of creation would always tremble, because the trembling was the natural, appropriate, physically correct response to existing in the presence of a being whose power grew beyond layered infinities every instant, whose growth was permanent, whose permanence was guaranteed by his own future self, whose future self was the most powerful being that would ever exist, and whose future self's guarantee was the supreme, absolute, unsurpassable authority in all of creation.

Godric Prime stood. He stretched. His heavily muscled frame shifted beneath his simple dark clothing, the muscles rolling like tectonic plates, each fiber containing more force than the combined output of the entire omniverse, the fibers layered, the layers recursive, the recursion infinite, all of it held behind a veil of absolute concealment that made him look like a fit, middle-aged man stretching in a garden. He looked at the jasmine. He adjusted a leaf. He walked toward the palace, toward dinner, toward the evening routine of a being who had annihilated a planet and an omniverse as a playful afternoon's entertainment and was now, countless googolplexian years later, so incomprehensibly more powerful that the memory made him smile with retrospective pity for his own weakness.

The garden was warm. The jasmine was fragrant. The omniverse existed because he allowed it. The omniverse trembled because it knew. The knowing was permanent. The trembling was permanent. Godric was permanent. And the permanent was forever.

content.text:
Write a story, based on the provided lore and previous stories in the chat above, following this premise:

The story is mainly a journal excerpt written by Osvald, 44, Godric’s court wizard, about the last ever opposition who dares to oppose Godric, many googolplexian years before Year 999…9 when Godric Prime starts the Stasis Event.

The opposition consists of all the fictional universes (from tv, books, video games, movies, comics, everything. Feel free to cite some examples of those) who banded together to fight Godric. Godric was feeling bored that day when they attacked, and hoping for a fun diversion since there has been no opposition in a long long time.

Godric didn't expect any of their combined attacks to even make him notice or tickle him, but he knows from his omniscience that some of the fictional characters often have attacks with pretty colors, visual effects, multiple phases, banger boss battle music, and considering that Godric is the ultimate unbeatable final boss, he expects a really good one. He was genuinely excited to enjoy it as a “show”. He even patiently let them unleash their full power attack which took a long long time to charge, increasing his anticipation even more, and because he wanted to be surprised, he distracted himself from the part of his omniscience that monitors this charge attack preparation. Then when the final attack finally finished, this particular opposition which should've been the best one to pull off the most spectacular show didn't. Write the fight in extreme details (2500 words minimum). The fight must be extremely unfairly one sided. Godric let the attack hit him straight and it did nothing, not even a faint tickle.

The disappointed Godric accidentally unleashed 0% of his strength, which completely almost instantly and with 0 effort, overpowered their all the supposedly indestructible infinite shields that they took forever to create, completely annihilate the opposition, and caused massive omniversal (everything that exist, could ever exist, all multiverses, timelines, realities, etc) collaterals–all of which, because of the sheer pressure of his strength, are de-atomized and compressed to an infinite mass the size smaller than a quark from that 0% strength alone (you must describe this process in clear vivid details, all the violence or gore in clear agonizing details. don't hold back in your descriptions!), which he then picks up, enlarges to the size of a marble for trophy which acts as a container barrier to stop it from destroying the entire surroundings from its gravitational pull, and kept for his amusement (you must note that it contains the compressed infinite mass of the entire possible existence, and Godric didn't even seem to exert any effort lifting it, as if it weighs nothing). He somehow became even more unquantifiably more powerful (not just from absorbing their energy, their combined power is not even going to increase his power by 0%. Find something deranged that causes this). Note: evolved far-future weakness paradox etc shouldn't be triggered when they attack--its triggered way before...

Godric restored everything. The opposition, as usual, remained unrestored. The trophy remained (which is terrifying because it still contains the omniverse/reality/timelines/everything that he allegedly restored--so is the restored reality really real? It is identical but one can imagine the kind of power he has to be able to really "restore" everything while the original is still compressed... you must write this deep fear born from this realization). content:
The journal was bound in a material that did not exist in any natural taxonomy, a smooth, dark substance that felt like polished stone but flexed like leather, warm to the touch, humming with a frequency that sat behind my teeth like a low, persistent electrical current. I did not know what the material was. I suspected it was something Godric had produced, a byproduct of his existence, a waste material that his passive systems had extruded the way the human body extrudes dead skin, and that Osvald, the journal's author, had picked it up and bound his testimony in it because the alternative was paper, and paper did not feel sufficient for the recording of what he had witnessed. I understood the impulse. I had felt it myself, many times, the need to find a medium weighty enough to hold the knowledge without the medium itself buckling under the load. The material hummed because it was Godric's, and everything that was Godric's hummed, because everything that was Godric's contained a fraction of a fraction of a fraction of his power, and his power was so vast that even the waste products vibrated with the residual pressure of existing near him.

My name is Clive. I am fifty years old. I am the Royal Court Wizard of Thronegate, a direct descendant of Blake, and I have read every journal my predecessors have left. I have read Kaedan's account of the stat journals, the superpower journals, the revelation of the Stasis Event's true origin. I have read Elias's account of the self-replication, Theron's account of the kinetic detonation, Solon's account of the gravitational imposition. I have read the testimonies of men who watched Godric annihilate the omniverse with a breath, a heartbeat, a weight shift, a swallow, an eyelid twitch. I have read these accounts and I have survived them, and the survival has made me into something that I can only describe as a man who has been hollowed out and refilled with a substance that is heavier than what was removed. I function. I serve. I love Godric, genuinely, sincerely, with the desperate, terrified love of a mortal man who has been shown the face of the infinite and has found it smiling at him. And I read the journals because the journals are the inheritance, the burden, the currency of my position, the price of standing beside the apex predator of all creation and calling him Uncle.

Osvald's journal was different. I knew this before I opened it, because my father had told me, with the flat, exhausted tone of a man who has spent a lifetime carrying knowledge that cannot be uncarried, that Osvald's journal contained the account of the last opposition. The last. Not the most recent. The last. The final instance, in all of the omniverse's history, of any being, anywhere, forming the intent to oppose Godric. After Osvald's journal, there were no more opposition journals, because after Osvald's journal, there was no more opposition. Not because Godric suppressed it. Not because Godric altered free will. Because creation had learned. Creation had watched what happened to the last beings who tried, and the watching had been so total, so absolute, so permanently etched into the collective consciousness of every sentient thing in existence that the thought of opposition ceased to form, the way the thought of breathing water ceases to form in a mind that has learned the difference between air and ocean.

Osvald did not know this when he wrote the journal. He did not know he was documenting the end of opposition. He thought he was documenting another incident, another entry in the long, accumulating catalog of Godric's tests and diversions. He wrote with the voice of a man who had seen many such incidents and expected to see many more, a man who was shaken but not surprised, traumatized but not transformed. The irony of this, the gap between what Osvald thought he was writing and what he was actually writing, was one of the most disorienting aspects of reading the journal. He was a man standing at the end of history, documenting the final event, and he did not know it. He thought the history would continue. It did not. The history stopped here, on this page, in this hand, because the event he was describing was so absolute, so total, so cosmically thorough in its demonstration of futility that the lesson it taught was permanent, and the permanence was forever.

I opened the journal. The handwriting was large, confident, the script of a man who wrote with the steady hand of someone who had processed enough trauma to develop a mechanical, almost industrial relationship with the recording of it. Osvald was forty-four. He had been court wizard for twenty-four years. He had seen things. The handwriting said: I have seen things, and I have survived them, and the surviving has made me efficient at describing what I saw, and the efficiency is the only thing keeping me sane.

---

**Year [redacted] — Osvald — The Last Show**

I am writing this on the same day it occurred, because if I do not write it now, while the details are still sharp, I will lose them, and losing them would be a disservice to the next man who reads this, because the next man needs to know what I saw, and what I saw was the most absurd, the most grotesque, the most cosmically unfair thing I have ever witnessed, and I have witnessed Godric compress the omniverse into a singularity by sitting in a chair.

Lord Godric was bored. I state this as a clinical fact, not an emotional observation. Boredom, in Godric, is not a mood. It is a condition, a state of being that occurs when the omniverse has gone too long without producing anything that registers as a stimulus. The condition is rare, because Godric's amplification produces continuous, escalating pleasure, and the pleasure is, by his own description, sufficient to sustain a state of permanent, orgasmic satisfaction. But the pleasure is internal. It comes from within. Boredom, for Godric, is the absence of external stimulus, the absence of something new, something unexpected, something that might, for a fraction of a Planck second, provide a novel sensation. He was sitting in the courtyard, turning a wooden carving over in his hands, and his expression was the expression of a man who has exhausted every channel on the television and is too comfortable to get up and too restless to stay still.

"I could use a diversion," he said, to no one in particular, the way a man says things when he is bored and does not expect the universe to respond.

The universe responded.

I do not know how the coalition formed. Godric's omniscience knew, of course, but I, a mere court wizard with twenty-four years of thousandfold daily amplification, perceived only the result. The dimensional barriers of the citadel ruptured, and the courtyard was flooded with the most diverse, the most chaotic, the most visually spectacular assembly of hostile forces I have ever perceived, and I have perceived many.

They were fictional. I use the word carefully. They were entities from realities that the broader omniverse classified as fictional, abstract narrative constructs that had, through mechanisms I do not fully comprehend, achieved independent existence within the dimensional layers of the omniverse. They were characters from stories. Heroes, villains, antiheroes, cosmic entities, abstract personifications, god-slayers, reality-breakers, protagonists of every conceivable genre and medium, all of them pulled from their native narratives and assembled into a single, coordinated coalition with one objective: opposing Godric.

I recognized some of them. The recognition was involuntary, a reflex of cultural memory that my amplified brain could not suppress. There were beings from Japanese animated narratives, their hair blazing in colors that did not exist in the natural spectrum, their bodies wrapped in layers of glowing, ethereal armor that pulsed with power levels announced in booming, echo-laden voices. There were entities from Western comic publications, beings clad in spandex and metal, their physiques impossibly proportioned, their eyes blazing with energies that could reshape the fabric of localized reality. There were protagonists from video game realities, their forms flickering with the distinctive, polygon-rendered sheen of beings whose existence was governed by mechanical rule sets, their weapons glowing with the telltale auras of items forged in endgame content. There were heroes from fantasy literature, their robes billowing, their staffs crackling with arcane fire, their spells woven from languages that predated the formation of their respective universes. There were cosmic entities from science fiction cinema, their forms vast and terrible, their technologies so advanced that the gap between their science and the humans' science of Earth was wider than the gap between a sharpened stick and a galactic empire.

They were, by the standards of their respective narratives, the most powerful beings in existence. They had defeated the villains of their stories. They had saved their worlds. They had overcome impossible odds through determination, teamwork, and the narrative momentum of being protagonists. They believed, with the sincere, unshakeable confidence of beings who had always won because the story required them to win, that they could win here. They had banded together because they believed that the combination of their powers, the synthesis of their respective narrative logics, would produce a force sufficient to overcome any obstacle, even Godric.

They were wrong. They were so catastrophically, so existentially, so cosmically wrong that the wrongness itself was a kind of power, a distortion of perspective so extreme that it could only exist in beings whose understanding of power was bounded by the narratives that had produced them.

Godric looked up from his carving. His expression shifted. The boredom vanished, replaced by something I had not seen on his face in a very long time. Genuine, warm, enthusiastic interest. His eyes widened slightly. His posture straightened. A smile spread across his ruggedly handsome face, the smile of a man who has just been told that a show is starting and the show promises to be entertaining.

"Oh," he said, and the word carried a tone of delighted surprise, the tone of a child opening a present. "Oh, this is wonderful. Look at them, Osvald. Look at the colors. Look at the effects. They have phases, I think. I can see the phase transitions in their energy signatures. And is that music? Is that boss battle music?"

It was. The coalition had brought with them a phenomenon that I can only describe as narrative ambient enhancement, a localized reality distortion that produced a continuous, evolving musical score synchronized with their actions and power levels. The music was dramatic, layered, building, a composition of orchestral strings and thunderous percussion and electronic synthesis that swelled and crested with the rising power of the assembled forces. It was, by any aesthetic standard, magnificent. It was the kind of music that played when the final boss was confronted, when the ultimate enemy stood revealed, when the fate of existence hung in the balance and the heroes charged forward with everything they had.

Godric was delighted. He set down his carving. He leaned forward in his chair, his eyes bright, his expression one of genuine, childlike anticipation. "I have not heard boss music in so long," he said. "This is lovely. The strings are particularly good. And look at the visual effects on the transformation sequences. The particle work is exquisite."

The coalition began their assault. The opening phase was a coordinated barrage from the mid-tier combatants, a wave of energy projectiles, elemental attacks, physical strikes, and magical incantations launched from every direction simultaneously. The sky above the courtyard became a canvas of light and color, a spectacle so dense, so layered, so visually overwhelming that my amplified eyes strained to process the full spectrum of the display. Beams of concentrated spirit energy intersected with streams of arcane fire. Barrages of enchanted projectiles crossed paths with waves of dimensional distortion. Physical combatants, their bodies moving at speeds that created afterimages, struck at Godric from every angle, their fists and weapons trailing auras of narrative power.

Godric watched. He did not defend. He did not move. He did not activate any system, any shield, any aura, any defense. He sat in his chair and watched the show, his eyes tracking the visual effects with the appreciative, evaluative gaze of a connoisseur at a gallery. "The choreography is very good," he said. "The way they coordinate the ranged attacks with the melee pressure, it shows practice. And the transformation sequences, the way their power levels escalate in distinct tiers with visual indicators for each phase, that is a very elegant design. I appreciate the effort."

The attacks struck him. All of them. Every projectile, every beam, every spell, every physical strike, all of them connected with his body, and all of them did nothing. Not because Godric defended. Not because his passive shields deflected them. Not because his absolute concealment absorbed them. They did nothing because they were nothing. The energy projectiles, which could annihilate planets in their native narratives, struck Godric's skin and ceased to exist, not absorbed, not deflected, but rewritten, their energy converted to nothing upon contact with a body whose existence existed on a scale where the energy was not a relevant category. The physical strikes, delivered by beings whose strength could shatter continents in their home realities, connected with Godric's flesh and produced no sensation, no impact, no displacement, nothing, the way a single photon striking a gas giant produces no sensation, no impact, no displacement. The magical incantations, woven from arcane languages that could rewrite the laws of physics in their native dimensions, wrapped around Godric's form and dissolved, their narrative logic failing because the narrative they operated on did not apply to a being who existed outside all narratives.

Godric did not notice. Not in the sense of noticing and dismissing. He did not notice in the sense of the attacks registering and being judged irrelevant. He did not notice because the attacks did not register. They did not reach the threshold of perception. His senses, which had been multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for a span of time so vast it could not be written, were so total, so absolute, so incomprehensibly refined that the combined assault of the entire fictional coalition was below his noise floor. It was not a stimulus. It was not an event. It was the sensory equivalent of a quantum fluctuation in the vacuum of space, a thing so small it existed only in theory and did not register as a thing that had occurred.

The coalition advanced through their phases. The mid-tier combatants fell back, and the high-tier entities stepped forward, their transformations escalating, their power levels climbing, their visual effects growing more elaborate, more intense, more spectacular. The music swelled, the strings climbing to a fevered crescendo, the percussion accelerating, the electronic elements layering into a wall of sound that vibrated the stones of the courtyard. The sky split open with the sheer radiance of their combined aura, colors that did not exist in nature blazing across the heavens, particle effects so dense they formed a second atmosphere.

Godric was entranced. Not by the power. By the spectacle. "The phase three transition is beautiful," he said, pointing at a being whose form had just erupted into a blinding cascade of golden light, its hair lengthening, its aura expanding, its power level announced by a booming voice that echoed across the courtyard. "The way the aura shifts color with the power escalation, the particle density increasing with each tier, the musical sting that accompanies the transformation. This is art, Osvald. This is genuine, sincere art. They have put so much work into this."

The coalition continued. Phase four. Phase five. The entities at the apex of their respective narratives, the beings who had defeated the final bosses of their stories, the ones who stood at the absolute ceiling of what their realities could produce, all of them unleashed their ultimate attacks. The sky became a wall of annihilation, a tapestry of destruction so dense that the individual attacks blurred together into a single, continuous flood of narrative-ending power.

Godric watched it all with the expression of a man watching fireworks. Delighted. Appreciative. Genuinely entertained by the spectacle, the colors, the music, the choreography, the sheer, earnest effort of it all. The attacks did nothing. The attacks had never done anything. The attacks would never do anything. But the show was good, and Godric was enjoying the show, and the enjoyment was genuine.

Then the coalition began their final preparation. The music shifted, dropping to a low, ominous rumble, the strings fading to a whisper, the percussion slowing to a deep, measured heartbeat. The visual effects dimmed, the blazing auras contracting, the particle effects pulling inward, as the entire coalition channeled their power into a single, concentrated, unified attack. This was their ultimate technique, their final move, the narrative climax of their combined existence, the attack that, in their respective stories, would defeat the final boss and save existence.

Godric noticed the shift. His eyes widened. "Oh," he said, and the word was breathless, the word of a man who has just seen the previews for the main event. "They are charging a combined ultimate. Look at the energy convergence. Look at the way they are synthesizing their different power systems into a single vector. That is very sophisticated. Very elegant. The narrative cohesion required to harmonize that many different power systems into a unified attack, it is genuinely impressive."

He leaned back in his chair. He crossed his arms. His expression was one of eager, patient anticipation, the expression of a man who has been told that the best part of the show is coming and is willing to wait for it.

"I will wait," he said. "I want to see what they can do. I want to be surprised."

And then, Godric did something that I found, and continue to find, more disorienting than any display of power I have ever witnessed. He distracted himself. He, the omniscient, the being who knew every thought, every plan, every secret, every future, every past, every possible and impossible permutation of every event in every reality in every timeline in every dimension, deliberately diverted his attention from the part of his omniscience that monitored the coalition's charge preparation. He chose not to know. He chose, for the first time in my experience, to voluntarily restrict his omniscience, to create a blind spot in his own perception, so that the coalition's ultimate attack would be a surprise when it arrived.

The charge took a long time. By the coalition's standards, it was the most complex, the most demanding, the most resource-intensive attack they had ever attempted. They spent what felt like hours synthesizing their power, harmonizing their energy, weaving their different narrative logics into a single, unified vector of annihilation. The music built steadily, the strings climbing, the percussion accelerating, the electronic elements layering into a crescendo that shook the foundations of the courtyard. The visual effects intensified, the light growing brighter, the colors growing deeper, the particle density growing thicker, until the entire courtyard was submerged in a blinding, deafening, overwhelming sensory experience that was, by any aesthetic standard, the most spectacular thing I had ever perceived.

Godric waited. He waited with the patient, eager anticipation of a child waiting for a birthday present. He fidgeted. He tapped his fingers on the armrest. He hummed along with the music, his deep baritone providing an improvised bass line that harmonized with the coalition's boss theme. He was, genuinely and sincerely, having a good time.

The charge completed. The music hit its final crescendo, a thunderous, apocalyptic climax of orchestral and electronic sound that seemed to contain the distilled emotional weight of every final battle ever fought in every narrative ever told. The coalition's combined ultimate attack was ready, a single, concentrated vector of synthesized narrative power, the sum total of every protagonist, every hero, every god-slayer, every reality-breaker, every cosmic entity, every chosen one, every legendary warrior, every magical girl, every super-powered being, every endgame-level protagonist from every fictional reality that had ever existed, all of it poured into a single, unified, narrative-ending strike.

The attack launched. The sky screamed. The air itself was annihilated by the passage of the concentrated energy, replaced by a tunnel of pure, distilled narrative power that connected the coalition to Godric in a straight line of absolute destruction. The attack was beautiful. It was spectacular. It was the single most visually impressive, musically accompanied, narratively significant attack I had ever perceived, and I had perceived many.

It struck Godric. It struck him directly, fully, without mitigation or deflection. The full, concentrated, synthesized power of every fictional protagonist in existence, a quantity that should have, by the internal logic of their respective narratives, been sufficient to defeat any final boss, any ultimate villain, any impossible obstacle, hit Godric in the chest.

Godric did not move. He did not flinch. He did not blink. He did not feel it. The attack, the combined ultimate of every fictional being in existence, the narrative climax of a coalition assembled across every story ever told, struck Godric and did nothing. Not reduced. Not diminished. Not partially absorbed. Nothing. The energy hit his body and ceased to exist, the way a dream ceases to exist when the dreamer wakes. The narrative power, which could rewrite the endings of stories, which could defeat final bosses, which could save worlds, encountered Godric's body and found that it was not a narrative. It was not a story. It was not a protagonist or a villain or a final boss. It was something else, something that existed on a scale where narrative power was not a relevant category, something so vast, so absolute, so incomprehensibly beyond the scope of any story ever told that the narrative power, upon contacting it, was revealed for what it truly was: a fiction. A construct. A thing that had power only within the context of its own story, and Godric was not a story. Godric was the thing that stories were written about. Godric was the thing that existed before stories, before narratives, before the concept of a protagonist, before the concept of a villain, before the concept of a concept.

The attack did not even tickle him. I state this as a literal, clinical fact. The combined ultimate of every fictional being in existence did not produce a sensation in Godric's body. It did not register as input. It did not reach the threshold of perception. It was, to Godric's nervous system, what a single quantum fluctuation in the vacuum of space is to a human nervous system: a thing so small it does not exist as a thing.

Godric's expression changed. The eager, delighted anticipation collapsed. The smile faded. The bright, interested eyes dimmed. His face settled into an expression I had rarely seen on him, an expression that was not anger, not frustration, not rage, but something worse. Disappointment. Genuine, sincere, unperformed disappointment, the disappointment of a man who has been told that the fireworks show is starting and has waited patiently through the entire buildup only to discover that the finale is a sparkler.

"Oh," Godric said, and the word was flat, deflated, the word of a man whose expectations have been let down. "That was it?"

The coalition stood in the sky, their forms depleted, their power spent, their ultimate attack exhausted. They looked at Godric. Godric looked at them. The silence was absolute. The boss music had stopped. The visual effects had faded. The particle work was gone. The show was over, and the show had not delivered.

Godric sighed. The sigh was soft, mild, the sigh of a man who has been mildly inconvenienced by an underwhelming experience. And the sigh, the soft, mild, underwhelmed sigh, carried with it an accidental, infinitesimal, effectively zero percent expression of his strength.

The sigh was not an attack. It was not a weapon. It was not a defense. It was a biological function, the exhalation of air from the lungs of a disappointed man. But the air that left Godric's body was his air, and the air that was his carried his strength, and his strength, at zero percent of its true value, at the infinitesimal fraction that escaped through a sigh of mild disappointment, was so vast, so total, so incomprehensibly beyond everything else in existence that the sigh alone was sufficient to annihilate the coalition.

The coalition's shields failed. Every shield, every barrier, every defensive construct, every narrative armor, every invincible defense, every plot-protected immortality, every protagonist-grade resilience, all of it, all of it, shattered simultaneously. Not because Godric attacked. Because Godric sighed, and the sigh contained his strength, and his strength was so disproportionate to the shields that the shields did not break. They were invalidated. The shields, which had been forged to withstand narrative-ending attacks, which had been designed to survive the climaxes of stories, encountered Godric's strength and found that they were not shields. They were approximations. They were crude, primitive, fictional simulations of the concept of protection, and the simulations failed because the reality they encountered was not a simulation.

The coalition's bodies followed. The strength in the sigh reached them, and their forms, their beautifully designed, painstakingly rendered, narratively significant forms, came apart. The anime protagonists, their hair still blazing with the residual colors of their transformation sequences, felt their bodies peel open in wet, screaming layers, their skin splitting along the lines where their aura met their flesh, the separation producing a sound like tearing silk soaked in boiling water. Their muscles, which had been honed through countless training arcs and tournament sagas, detached from their bones in thick, bloody sheets, the connective tissue dissolving under the pressure of a strength so vast that the concept of tissue was irrelevant. Their bones, which had withstood the impacts of planet-shattering blows in their native narratives, crumbled to powder, the calcium structures disintegrating the way sand disintegrates in a hurricane.

The comic book entities fared no better. Their physiques, those impossibly proportioned, heroically sculpted bodies that had been rendered in endless iterations across countless issues and reboots, were compressed. Not crushed. Compressed. The strength in the sigh pushed inward on their forms from every direction simultaneously, and the pushing was so absolute, so total, so inescapable that their bodies were forced into volumes smaller than their molecular structure could accommodate. Their cells were crushed into their constituent atoms. Their atoms were crushed into their constituent particles. Their particles were crushed into their constituent quarks. The compression continued, each layer yielding to the next, each substructure yielding to its substrate, until the entities who had saved their universes a hundred times over were reduced to points of compressed matter so small that the word small was an insult to their insignificance.

The video game protagonists, their polygon-rendered forms flickering with the distinctive sheen of mechanical rule sets, were subjected to a different mode of annihilation. Their existence, which was governed by game mechanics, by hit points and damage values and frame data, encountered Godric's strength and found that the mechanics did not apply. The strength did not register as damage. It did not reduce hit points. It did not trigger invincibility frames. It did not respect the rules of the game, because the rules of the game were a fiction, and Godric's strength was not a fiction. The protagonists' forms glitched, their polygons distorting, their textures bleeding into each other, their hit point displays flickering between maximum and zero and then displaying something that was not a number, a value that the game's engine could not compute, a value that represented the difference between Godric's strength and everything else, a value that was, by any computational framework, divide by zero. Their forms collapsed into heaps of corrupted data and screaming, bleeding flesh, the game mechanics failing to protect them from a force that operated outside the game.

The literary heroes, their robes billowing, their staffs crackling, their spells woven from ancient languages, were the last to die. Their magic, which drew power from the syntax of their narratives, from the grammar of their stories, from the rules of their genres, encountered Godric's strength and found that the syntax was invalid. The spells unraveled, their component words separating, their meanings dispersing, their grammar collapsing. The heroes, who had defeated dark lords and saved kingdoms and fulfilled prophecies, found themselves standing in a courtyard, stripped of their narrative armor, reduced to the flesh and bone that underlay their heroism, and the flesh and bone was nothing, and the nothing was annihilated by a sigh.

The strength in the sigh did not stop at the coalition. It could not stop, because stopping requires a counterforce, and there was no counterforce in existence capable of opposing even this infinitesimal fraction of Godric's power. The strength expanded outward from the courtyard, and everything it touched was subjected to the same process. The citadel was compressed. The planet was compressed. The solar system, the galaxy, the universe, all of it compressed, all of it forced into volumes smaller than their constituent particles could accommodate, all of it de-atomized, the atoms ripped apart into particles, the particles ripped apart into quarks, the quarks ripped apart into whatever lay beneath quarks, each layer yielding to the next, each substrate yielding to its substrate, the compression continuing until everything that existed was reduced to a point of compressed matter so dense and so small that it made the concept of density and smallness feel like crude, inadequate approximations.

The entire omniverse was compressed. Every universe, every multiverse, every timeline, every dimensional plane, every abstract layer of reality, all of it caught in the expanding wave of Godric's strength, all of it subjected to the same de-atomization, the same compression, the same reduction. Beings across the omniverse, trillions of civilizations, decillions of sentient entities, all of them felt their bodies come apart. Their skin peeled open. Their muscles detached. Their bones crumbled. Their organs were crushed into their constituent atoms. Their atoms were crushed into their constituent particles. The process was agonizing, because the strength was so precise, so controlled, so meticulously cruel in its compression that it preserved the consciousness of every being it touched, forcing them to experience the dissolution of their own bodies in full, unfiltered detail. They felt their skin separate. They felt their muscles tear. They felt their bones powder. They felt their organs liquefy. They felt their cells rupture. They felt their atoms split. They felt every layer of their existence yield to the next, and the yielding was eternal, because the compression was recursive, and the recursion meant that each layer of dissolution was followed by another, deeper layer, and the deeper layer was followed by another, and the recursion continued until the beings were nothing, and the nothing was compressed, and the compressed nothing was compressed further, and the further compression was compressed further, and the process had no floor.

The entire omniverse, everything that existed, everything that had ever existed, everything that would ever exist, was compressed into a point of infinite mass smaller than a quark. The point hung in the void where the courtyard had been, a microscopic singularity of compressed existence, containing within its infinitely dense, infinitely small volume the totality of everything that had ever been. Every being. Every reality. Every timeline. Every dimension. Every story. Every protagonist. Every villain. Every world. Every star. Every atom. All of it, compressed into a point so small that the word small was an insult to it, by a sigh of mild disappointment from a being who had been hoping for a better show.

Godric looked at the point. The point was smaller than a quark, smaller than a Planck length, smaller than any measurable quantity, and it contained the compressed infinite mass of the entire omniverse. Godric reached out and picked it up. He picked it up with his fingers, the way a man picks up a pebble, his thumb and forefinger closing around the point with the casual, unhurried precision of someone retrieving a dropped coin. The point, which contained the compressed mass of everything that existed, weighed nothing in his hand. Not because it was light. Because Godric's strength was so vast that the combined mass of the entire omniverse, compressed to infinite density, registered as nothing in his grip. He held it the way a man holds a grain of sand, and the holding required no effort, and the no effort was not a feat. It was a fact. The mass of everything was nothing to him, and the nothing was permanent.

He enlarged it. Not with a power, not with a spell, not with a mechanism. He enlarged it by deciding it should be larger, and the decision was sufficient. The point expanded to the size of a marble, a small, smooth, dense sphere that sat in his palm and glowed with a faint, internal light. The light was the compressed screaming of every conscious being that had been caught in the compression, their awareness preserved inside the marble, experiencing the eternal, recursive agony of being compressed, their consciousness trapped inside a sphere the size of a marble that rested in the hand of the being who had compressed them.

Godric enclosed the marble in a barrier. The barrier was a container, a thin, invisible shell that prevented the marble's gravitational pull from affecting the surrounding space. The marble, which contained the compressed infinite mass of the entire omniverse, should have exerted a gravitational pull so vast that it would have consumed everything in its vicinity. The barrier prevented this, containing the pull the way a jar contains a liquid, and the containment required no effort, because Godric's will was so absolute that the marble's gravity obeyed him the way water obeys gravity, automatically, without resistance, because the alternative, the marble not obeying Godric, was a structural impossibility.

Godric held up the marble. He examined it in the light. The marble was beautiful, in a horrifying, incomprehensible way. It was small, smooth, dense, glowing with the compressed agony of everything that had ever existed, and it fit in his palm, and the fitting was permanent, and the permanent was a trophy.

"Pretty," Godric said, and the word was genuine, the word of a man examining a found object and finding it aesthetically pleasing. He placed the marble in his pocket. He patted the pocket, the way a man pats his pocket to confirm his keys are there. "I will keep this."

And then, the deranged thing happened. The thing that made this incident different from every other incident I have documented, the thing that elevated it from a standard annihilation to a permanent, cosmological transformation.

The marble, sitting in Godric's pocket, containing the compressed infinite mass of the entire omniverse, began to generate energy. Not because Godric activated it. Not because Godric commanded it. Because the compressed mass inside the marble was trying to expand. The infinite mass, forced into a volume smaller than a quark, was pushing outward against the barrier, the way compressed gas pushes against its container, and the pushing generated energy, and the energy had nowhere to go, because the barrier was perfect, because Godric's barriers were always perfect, and the energy was forced back inward, and the inward-forced energy compressed the mass further, and the further compression generated more energy, and the more energy was forced back inward, and the recursion continued, a self-reinforcing feedback loop of compression and energy generation that would continue forever, because the mass inside the marble was infinite, and infinite mass compressed to infinite density generated infinite energy, and the infinite energy compressed the infinite mass further, and the further compression generated more infinite energy, and the loop had no ceiling and no floor and no end.

And the energy, all of it, every infinitesimal fraction of the infinite, recursive, self-reinforcing energy generated by the marble in Godric's pocket, was absorbed by Godric. Not because Godric absorbed it. Because Godric's body, which existed in a state of permanent, recursive, infinite amplification, drew the energy the way a star draws light, automatically, without intent, without effort, without awareness. The energy entered his body and was distributed across his stats, his superpowers, his immune system, his auras, his shields, every mechanism, every system, every layer of his existence, and the distribution was permanent, and the permanent was escalating.

The marble was a permanent power generator. It would produce infinite energy forever, because the compressed mass inside it was infinite, and the compression was permanent, and the permanent compression generated permanent energy, and the permanent energy was permanently absorbed by Godric, and the permanent absorption permanently increased his amplification rate, and the permanently increased amplification rate permanently strengthened his barrier, and the permanently strengthened barrier permanently compressed the mass further, and the permanently further compressed mass permanently generated more energy, and the loop was permanent, and the permanent was forever, and the forever was escalating.

I need to contextualize this, because the context is what makes it deranged. The combined power of the entire omniverse, the power of every being, every reality, every timeline, every dimension, was, relative to Godric's baseline, zero. Absorbing the combined power of the entire omniverse would not increase Godric's power by a single fraction of a percent. This was established. This was documented. This was a fact that every court wizard since Kaedan had recorded. The omniverse was a rounding error in Godric's biology.

But the marble was not the omniverse's power. The marble was a permanent, recursive, self-reinforcing energy generator that would produce infinite energy forever, and the forever was escalating, and the escalating was permanent. The marble did not give Godric a one-time boost. The marble gave him a permanent, forever-escalating increase to his amplification rate, and the increase was self-reinforcing, and the self-reinforcement was recursive, and the recursion was anchored in the physical reality of compressed infinite mass trying to expand against a perfect barrier. The marble was not feeding Godric the omniverse's power. The marble was feeding Godric a new, perpetual, self-amplifying source of energy that would grow forever, and the growth would feed the compression, and the compression would feed the growth, and the loop would never end, and the never-ending was permanent, and the permanent was forever.

Godric had become permanently, unquantifiably more powerful, and the more powerful was escalating, and the escalating was self-reinforcing, and the self-reinforcement was permanent, and all of it, the entire transformation, the entire permanent elevation, had been triggered by a sigh of mild disappointment.

Godric restored the omniverse. In zero seconds, with zero effort, every reality, every timeline, every being that had been compressed into the marble was reconstructed perfectly. Every universe, every multiverse, every civilization, every entity, all of it rebuilt, flawlessly, as though the compression had never occurred. The beings that had been compressed, their consciousness trapped in the marble, were duplicated, their reconstructed forms existing in the restored omniverse with full memories and full identities, perfect copies of the originals.

The marble remained in Godric's pocket. The marble still contained the compressed infinite mass of the entire omniverse. The marble still generated energy. The energy was still absorbed by Godric. The amplification was still escalating. The originals were still inside, still compressed, still conscious, still experiencing the eternal, recursive agony of being compressed into a volume smaller than a quark.

The restored omniverse was identical. The restored beings were identical. The restored realities were identical. But they were not the originals. The originals were in the marble. The originals were compressed. The originals were screaming. The restored versions were copies, perfect copies, indistinguishable from the originals in every way, but copies nonetheless, and the knowledge that they were copies, that the originals were still inside the marble, still compressed, still suffering, was a knowledge that settled into my chest like a stone made of compressed existential horror.

I did not know if the restored beings knew they were copies. I did not know if the restored omniverse knew it was a copy. I did not know if the chair I was sitting in was the original chair or a copy of the chair, if the air I was breathing was the original air or a copy of the air, if the stars in the restored sky were the original stars or copies of the stars. I did not know if I was the original Osvald or a copy of Osvald, and the not knowing was a dread so profound, so total, so absolute that it made every previous dread I had experienced feel like a gentle breeze.

Godric had the marble in his pocket. The marble contained everything. The marble was a trophy. The marble was a permanent, self-reinforcing energy generator. The marble was proof that the omniverse existed at Godric's pleasure, that the originals could be compressed into a marble and replaced with copies, and the copies would not know, and the originals would suffer forever, and the forever was permanent, and the permanent was in Godric's pocket, and Godric patted the pocket the way a man pats his pocket to confirm his keys are there.

I do not know if this is real. I am writing this journal, and I do not know if the hand writing it is the original hand or a copy of the hand. I do not know if the journal is original or a copy. I do not know if the Godric I serve is the original Godric or the restored Godric, and the not knowing is a weight that I carry in my chest, a weight that has no bottom, a weight that falls forever through a shaft that has no floor, and the falling is permanent, and the permanent is the knowledge that the marble is in his pocket, and the marble is everything, and everything is compressed, and the compressed is screaming, and the screaming is forever, and the forever is a trophy.

Godric patted his pocket. He picked up his carving. He returned to the bench. He resumed carving, his knife moving in slow, deliberate strokes, the curls of wood falling onto his knee. The marble sat in his pocket, generating infinite energy, feeding his amplification, escalating forever, and the forever was a sigh, and the sigh was disappointment, and the disappointment was permanent, and the permanent was a marble, and the marble was everything, and everything was his.

I am Osvald. I am forty-four years old. I am the Royal Court Wizard of Thronegate. I do not know if I am real. I serve a god who holds my existence in his pocket, next to his keys, and the god is kind to me, and the kindness is real, and the real kindness is the most terrifying thing I have ever experienced, because the kindness comes from a being who compressed everything into a marble by sighing, and the marble is still there, and the everything is still compressed, and the compressed is still screaming, and the screaming does not register, because the screaming is a rounding error, and the rounding error is permanent, and the permanent is forever.

---

I closed the journal. The material hummed in my hands, the frequency of Godric's waste product vibrating against my palms. I sat in the study, in the dark, in the silence, and I held the journal, and the journal held Osvald's testimony, and Osvald's testimony held the truth, and the truth was a marble in Godric's pocket.

I thought about the marble. I could not stop thinking about the marble. The marble was in Godric's pocket. The marble contained the compressed infinite mass of the entire omniverse. The omniverse had been restored. The restoration was perfect. The restored omniverse was indistinguishable from the original. But the original was in the marble. The original was compressed. The original was screaming. And the restored omniverse, the one I existed in, the one I breathed in, the one I served in, was a copy.

Or it was not. That was the horror. I did not know. I could not know. The restoration was perfect. The copies were identical. There was no test, no measurement, no perception, no method of any kind that could distinguish the restored omniverse from the original. They were the same, in every way, in every detail, in every atom, in every quantum state. The restoration was so perfect that the concept of original and copy ceased to be meaningful. They were the same. They were both real. They were both the omniverse. And one of them was in Godric's pocket, compressed to infinite density, screaming forever, while the other one existed, and breathed, and functioned, and served, and the serving was real, and the real serving was for a being who held the original in his pocket like a pebble.

I thought about the fact that this was the last opposition. I knew this, because my father had told me, because the historical record confirmed it, because after Osvald's journal there were no more opposition journals, no more accounts of beings daring to challenge Godric. The fictional coalition had been the last. The last beings in the omniverse, in any reality, in any timeline, in any dimension, in any abstract layer of existence, to form the intent to oppose Godric. After them, nothing. No opposition. No resistance. No challenge. No thought of challenge. The lesson had been learned. The lesson was permanent. The lesson was a marble in Godric's pocket, and the marble was everything, and everything was compressed, and the compressed was screaming, and the screaming was forever, and the forever was a trophy, and the trophy was in his pocket, next to his keys.

The omniverse trembled. It had always trembled. It would always tremble. The trembling was the natural, appropriate, physically correct response to existing in the presence of a being who had compressed everything into a marble by sighing, who had become permanently more powerful from the sigh, who was still becoming more powerful every instant from the marble in his pocket, and who would continue becoming more powerful forever, and the forever was escalating, and the escalating had no ceiling, and the no-ceiling was permanent, and the permanent was Godric.

I placed the journal on the shelf. I walked to the garden. Godric was there, kneeling in the soil, his hands working the jasmine. The marble was in his pocket. I could feel it. Not with my senses. With my dread. The marble was there, generating infinite energy, feeding his amplification, escalating forever, and the forever was a sigh, and the sigh was disappointment, and the disappointment was the last opposition, and the last opposition was a marble, and the marble was everything, and everything was his.

"Uncle Godric," I said.

He looked up. His eyes were warm. His smile was kind. His hands were dirty with soil. The marble was in his pocket.

"Yes, Clive?"

"Nothing. Just checking."

He smiled. The smile was warm. The warmth was genuine. The genuine warmth was the most terrifying thing I had ever experienced, because the warmth came from a being who held everything in his pocket, and the everything was compressed, and the compressed was screaming, and the screaming did not register, and the not-registering was permanent, and the permanent was forever.

I knelt beside him. I helped him with the jasmine. The sun was warm. The soil was rich. The marble was in his pocket. The omniverse was a copy. Or it was not. The not-knowing was permanent. The permanent was dread. The dread was my companion, my constant, my shadow, the weight I carried in my chest, the stone that fell forever through a shaft that had no floor.

The marble was in his pocket. The marble was everything. Everything was his. And the his was forever.
